Title: TechCrunch Mobility: Travis Kalanick's return proves it really is 2016 again | TechCrunch
URL: https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/15/techcrunch-mobility-travis-kalanicks-return-proves-it-really-is-2016-again/
Time Published: 2026-03-15T16:07:33Z
Full Content:
Latest AI Amazon Apps Biotech & Health Climate Cloud Computing Commerce Crypto Enterprise EVs Fintech Fundraising Gadgets Gaming Google Government & Policy Hardware Instagram Layoffs Media & Entertainment Meta Microsoft Privacy Robotics Security Social Space Startups TikTok Transportation Venture Staff Events Startup Battlefield StrictlyVC Newsletters Podcasts Videos Partner Content TechCrunch Brand Studio Crunchboard Contact Us Welcome back to TechCrunch Mobility — your central hub for news and insights on the future of transportation. To get this in your inbox, sign up here for free — just click TechCrunch Mobility! This newsletter was wrapped and ready to go and then Travis Kalanick brought me back to 2016 by making an eyebrow-raising announcement. Uber’s co-founder and former CEO who resigned in 2017 after a string of controversies is back and building a robotics company called Atoms. And, wait for it, he is on the precipice of acquiring Pronto, the autonomous vehicle startup focused on industrial and mining sites that was created by his former Uber colleague, Anthony Levandowski. Kalanick revealed he is already the “largest investor” in Pronto. That is a lot to digest and the final sign that, yes, we really are back in 2016. For those who may not remember, 2016 was a hyped year for AVs. Uber acquired Levandowski’s startup Otto — a deal that went sideways almost immediately and resulted in Waymo suing the ride-hailing company for trade secret theft. Now, back to our original programming. Just last week I waxed on about Rivian and the EV maker’s bid to make the upcoming R2 SUV one of the fastest vehicle launches in history. ICYMI, here it is. This week, I’m in Austin for SXSW — the annual tech meets music meets film, TV, and comedy festival. Rivian, the headline sponsor of SXSW, used the event to share pricing and other specs of its R2. There is still a lot to unpack and I’m still interviewing folks as I write this (including CEO RJ Scaringe later today), but here is what we know. The performance launch edition, which will be the first version of R2 on the line, will start at $57,990. My initial article digs into what you get for that price. And senior reporter Sean O’Kane focused on the long-promised $45,000 version and why it won’t be coming until late 2027. My time at SXSW with the Rivian folks has begun to reveal some of their R2 strategy. The company is leaning heavily into experiential marketing targeted directly at its core market. SXSW attendees in Austin, who include well-heeled tech and creative folks from all over world, are about as close as you can get to a Rivian customer archetype. But will it work? The EV itself is far more approachable IMO than the much larger and expensive flagship R1 truck and SUV. The R2 also has a few items, including a new operating system, that is more powerful and capable than its pricey peer. The operating system software, which admittedly I haven’t tested at length, is a standout improvement from the R1 in terms of computing and user interface. For instance, the R2 has one SoC (system on chip) that runs the infotainment and handles 200 TOPS (tera operations per second) of computing on the edge. The next-gen R1 vehicles have four SoC and do most of the computing in the cloud. Rivian’s head of software, Wassym Bensaid, told me this edge computing matters because it allows the company to run large language models locally, which will provide much lower latency and better performance. Another big change that I will briefly mention are the “halo wheels” on the steering wheel, which are pictured above. These wheels give haptic feedback and let the driver quickly change the temperature, fan speed, and speaker volume without moving their hands or eyes over to the central screen. Chief designer Jeff Hammoud told me this addressed some of the biggest requests from customers without adding a bunch of buttons. Notably, software allows the company to add more capability to these halo wheels over time. Lucid Motors tried to make a splash at its investor day when it showed a robotaxi concept designed to be built on the company’s “midsize” EV platform. Interim CEO Marc Winterhoff sounded declarative onstage, saying the company is “working on a dedicated Lucid Robotaxi” that would come after the midsize EVs debut. The company later clarified to TechCrunch that there is no active development happening and that the vehicle is just a concept. A little bird tells us the project is very new, having started only in the last two to three months, a timeline that leaves us wondering how long it would really take for Lucid Motors to put something like this on the road. Got a tip for us? Email Kirsten Korosec at kirsten.korosec@techcrunch.com or my Signal at kkorosec.07, or email Sean O’Kane at sean.okane@techcrunch.com. I guess this is the Rivian issue! Because the company has spun ANOTHER startup and raised a bunch of money to scale it up. I’m referring to Mind Robotics, an industrial robotics lab, and its $500 million Series A funding round that was co-led by venture firms Accel and Andreessen Horowitz. As Sean O’Kane wrote, the financing follows a $115 million seed round that was led by Eclipse (this VC really seems to be everywhere lately) in late 2025. Mind Robotics is now valued at around $2 billion. Robotics was a focus of some of Rivian’s programming at SXSW, including a panel with engineer and YouTuber Mark Rober and Rivian CEO RJ Scaringe. I interviewed Scaringe on the sidelines of the event and asked him about Mind Robotics and what was behind the effort. I also asked if he plans to spin out any more companies. Let’s just say there was a very long pause before he ultimately said, “Probably not.” I will have a longer story about the interview soon, but one item worth mentioning is that Scaringe basically believes companies are thinking about the future of industrial robotics all wrong. “There has been a surprising emphasis on mimicking human biomechanics, or in some cases even going further, making even more complex mechatronics. I think what’s missed in industrial [settings] and this is one of the things we really see clearly, is the work happens with the hands,” Scaringe told me. “So the hands are very, very important. Everything else, from a robotic system point of view, is to get the hands to the right place. And so the ability for the robots to do really complex motions, like, let’s say, a back flip, that actually just means the robot has a lot of unnecessary complexity in it for the vast majority of tasks and understanding.” Other deals that got my attention this week … Global ride-hailing company inDrive acquired Pakistan-based quick-commerce startup Krave Mart. The all-stock deal received approval from the Competition Commission of Pakistan, allowing the companies to proceed with the transaction, two sources familiar with the matter told TechCrunch. inDrive confirmed the acquisition but declined to disclose its financial terms. Mirai Robotics, an Italy-based company developing autonomous maritime systems, raised $4.2 million from Primo Capital, Techshop, and 40Jemz Ventures. Surf Air placed an order for 25 of Beta Technologies’ all-electric ALIA aircraft and acquired options for up to 75 more. Bryan Reimer, an MIT research scientist who was recently on my Autonocast podcast, poses an interesting question in a column published this month: Are automated vehicles headed for the same political divide as electric cars? Archer Aviation responded to a lawsuit with its own counterclaims that rival Joby Aviation allegedly defrauded the U.S. government and its competitors by falsely presenting itself as an American-made company. The electric air sector is getting litigious, which can be mighty distracting and expensive in this early stage of development. Group14 has started production of silicon battery materials at its BAM-3 factory in South Korea. The factory is capable of producing up to 2,000 metric tons annually, enough for 10 gigawatt-hours of energy storage, or about 100,000 long-range EVs. Hayden AI, a startup based in San Francisco, has sued its co-founder and former CEO over allegations of serious misconduct and misuse of funds. The Federal Aviation Administration OK’d eight pilot programs that will allow a handful of companies, including Archer Aviation, Beta Technologies, Joby Aviation, and Wisk, to start widespread electric aircraft testing as early as this summer. The three-year program will span 26 states! Harbinger, a Los Angeles-based EV startup, revealed its second vehicle: a smaller, medium-duty work truck. Lucid Motors is shipping Apple CarPlay and Android Auto to Gravity SUV owners. The National Transportation Safety Board released information that shows two drivers involved in fatal crashes in 2024 while using Ford’s BlueCruise hands-free driving system were likely distracted in the moments before impact. Nuro, the Silicon Valley-based startup backed by Nvidia, Uber, and SoftBank, is testing its autonomous vehicle technology in Japan. Slate Auto, the EV startup backed by Jeff Bezos, has a new CEO ahead of its production launch. Former Amazon Marketplace vice president Peter Faricy now leads the company, while former CEO Christine Barman is now president of vehicles. Tesla is now an officially licensed utility in the United Kingdom. Wayve is teaming up with Uber and Nissan to launch a robotaxi service in Tokyo, with a pilot scheduled for late 2026. Uber added Hyundai-owned Motional to its growing robotaxi network. Motional’s self-driving Hyundai Ioniq 5 vehicles now show up on the Uber app in Las Vegas. These vehicles have human safety monitors in the car, for now. Zoox had a couple of announcements this week that provide a roadmap of sorts of where the Amazon-owned company wants to go. The company has started to map the streets of Dallas and Phoenix, the first step before testing its autonomous vehicles in the Sun Belt cities. It is also partnering with Uber and will make its robotaxis available to hail on the ride-hailing app in Las Vegas later this year. That Uber tie-up deserves an important caveat, though. Zoox can’t deploy commercially until it gets an exemption from the Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standards for its custom-built robotaxis, which don’t have a steering wheel or other traditional controls. That process has started. The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration said it would begin taking public comment on Zoox’s application for those exemptions. Zoox only has an exemption to demonstrate its vehicles. The Chevrolet Bolt EV is coming back — a true EV deep cut. And senior reporter Tim De Chant went to a company-sponsored press drive and came back with an interesting idea that I have become more and more fixated on as an EV sales ploy. The Chevy Bolt is the McRib of the automotive world. Need I say more? De Chant also dug into the economics of GM bringing back the Bolt. Topics Transportation Editor Actively scaling? Fundraising? Planning your next launch?TechCrunch Founder Summit 2026 delivers tactical playbooks and direct access to 1,000+ founders and investors who are building, backing, and closing. Title: Exclusive -- GOP Strategist Caroline Wren: Ken Paxton Represents ‘Country over Party’ Politics You ‘Don’t See Anymore’
URL: https://www.breitbart.com/politics/2026/03/15/exclusive-gop-strategist-caroline-wren-ken-paxton-represents-country-over-party-politics-you-dont-see-anymore/
Time Published: 2026-03-15T14:25:58Z
Full Content:
GOP strategist Caroline Wren said on Breitbart News Saturday she opposes Texas Sen. John Cornyn’s bid for a fifth term, criticizing his record in the Senate and his past positions toward President Donald Trump while discussing the Republican primary runoff against Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton. Washington Bureau Chief Matthew Boyle introduced Wren by noting she has been “a woman on a mission exposing Texas Senator John Cornyn,” and asked, “Why is he looking for another term in the US Senate? What is he really up to? Who is this guy?” Wren responded that the issue is personal to her because she is “born and raised in Texas,” adding, “John Cornyn, to me, represents I think everything is wrong with the US Senate.” Wren argued Cornyn, who is “currently seeking his fifth term,” has “no actual accomplishments that he could point to, other than trying to restrict our Second Amendment rights,” while also contending that “in the last decade, this man has gone out of his way to attack Donald Trump and everything that America First stands for.” She said Cornyn would go “to MSNBC, CNN, C-span, NPR, all the outlets that despise Donald Trump,” and described him as “the last kind of stance in Texas from the old Bush guard.” She also cast Cornyn as “an institutionalist,” saying he believes “his colleagues and the Democrats, you know, respect and play by the same rules that he does.” Wren said Republicans “learned this at the Kavanaugh hearings,” adding, “The Democrats have declared war on us, not the other way around,” and arguing that “Donald Trump changed that game. He blew that game up, and John Cornyn is so out of step.” Wren further argued that a final term for Cornyn would create political risk for Trump, saying senators who no longer face voters often break with him. “This is why I think John Cornyn is particularly dangerous in giving him this last term,” she said, later adding, “Donald Trump’s endorsement is the most powerful endorsement in the country, and it is something that should be earned.” Wren said she does “not think John Cornyn has earned that endorsement.” Asked where the race stands after the primary, Wren said unseating an incumbent senator is historically rare, noting that “an incumbent U.S. senator has not lost their primary since 2002.” She said Cornyn and allied groups spent heavily during the race, explaining that “John Cornyn and related committees, the NRSC, something called Senate Leadership Fund, which is a super PAC that John Thune controls,” spent large sums attacking Paxton. Wren said “they spent hundreds of millions,” adding that “they spent 70 to 100 million, we’re not exactly sure, at least 70 million on TV, then another 30 million on digital.” She argued that an incumbent senator who “spent $100 million to tie Ken Paxton, who spent 5 million” demonstrated weakness, saying, “ that’s actually pathetic.” The strategist also described what she said was an internal fight over Trump’s potential endorsement. Wren said consultants tied to Trump’s political operation had urged him to support Cornyn by arguing “Ken Paxton will lose a general,” a case she called “preposterous.” She said Trump’s statement that he would make an endorsement “very soon” was followed by reports that he was set to back Cornyn, but that the situation shifted after more of Cornyn’s past comments surfaced and after Paxton responded by linking any decision to the SAVE AMERICA Act. “What Ken Paxton did was genius,” Wren said, adding that the move helped force Cornyn to change course by saying, “Okay, I believe I will support lifting the filibuster to get this passed.” Wren said Paxton’s move showed he was willing to put “your country over party,” adding that “you don’t see that anymore.” Wren said that, in her view, Cornyn is now struggling politically, arguing, “for the last 10 days, we’ve seen John Cornyn absolutely flailing.” She said the absence of newly released survey results is telling: “These pollsters in the Cornyn campaign, they have been in the field every single day … and they have not released a single poll. Why? Well, because I think those polls are devastating for Cornyn.” Wren pointed to Paxton’s move tying his campaign to passage of the SAVE AMERICA Act, calling it “genius” and saying it showed he was willing to put “your country over party,” adding, “you don’t see that anymore.” Wren said her message to the White House has been, “either one, stay out and just don’t endorse,” or “endorse Ken Paxton, who, frankly, deserves your endorsement.” Wren, who spent 15 years working as a political fundraiser and served as Trump’s “National Finance Director from 2016 through 2020,” said her experience with the Republican Attorneys General Association showed Paxton stood apart from other officeholders because he would not bend under donor pressure. “Their most hated person at RAGA … was Ken Paxton,” she said, recounting that lobbyists would approach him and “Ken Paxton would just say, ‘Thanks. Nice to see you.’ Get up and walk away, and he would double down on his lawsuit.” That, she argued, is “why Ken Paxton is so dangerous to the establishment.” Wren cited a series of legal actions she said Paxton has brought, including lawsuits against “Blackrock, Vanguard, and State Street,” Meta, Shein, Temu, major opioid companies, insulin manufacturers, and Eli Lilly, while also saying Paxton had “taken on the CCP.” She said those cases show “these senators, they schmooze in DC with these corporate PAC lobbyists who give them a $10,000 PAC check and say, Go vote one way,” but that “Ken Paxton is not going to do that.” Please let us know if you're having issues with commenting. Copyright © 2026 Breitbart

Title: Sam Altman Admits That AI Is Disrupting the Basic Fabric of Capitalism
URL: http://futurism.com/artificial-intelligence/sam-altman-ai-labor
Time Published: 2026-03-15T11:30:00Z
Full Content:
By Joe Wilkins Published Mar 15, 2026 7:30 AM EDT Sign up to see the future, today Can’t-miss innovations from the bleeding edge of science and tech OpenAI CEO Sam Altman’s public persona depends on a carefully balanced contradiction. Though he presents himself as the thoughtful steward of a dangerous technology, the AI luminary has aggressively pursued relationships with top lawmakers while racing rival tech moguls to the top of the market, consequences be damned. One of the ways he maintains this shtick is by acknowledging painful truths he has no plan of acting on. Speaking at the BlackRock Infrastructure Summit this week, Altman mused on AI’s public relations crisis, admitting that the tech is upending the dynamic between capital and labor. “Data centers are getting blamed for electricity prices hikes. Almost every company that does layoffs is blaming AI, whether or not it really is about AI,” Altman said, per Fortune, alluding to “AI washing” in which firms use the tech as cover for more ordinary market pressures driving workforce reductions. Whatever the cause, he granted that AI is letting “capital” — businesses owners — radically erode worker power as it takes us from an economy of scarcity to one of “abundance.” “So that’s, like, a real change to how capitalism has worked,” Altman said, acknowledging that the economic system has always relied on a delicate equilibrium between businesses owners and workers (though how balanced the two forces have actually been is certainly up for debate.) “But if it’s hard in many of our current jobs to outwork a GPU, then that changes. If there was an easy consensus answer, we’d have done it by now, so I don’t think anyone knows what to do.” Whether tactical or sincere, Altman’s observation is itself a clever bit of AI-washing. By stating the obvious — AI serves the ruling class in undermining worker power — Altman both frames the outcome as inevitable and washes his hands of responsibility. Case in point: OpenAI under Altman has made zero commitment to worker welfare; if anything, it’s done the opposite. There’s no support for sectoral bargaining in the tech industry, let alone any other industry, no advocacy for cost of living reductions that would help the working class survive the AI onslaught, and certainly no worker representation within OpenAI’s governance structure itself. Altman’s concern, in other words, is purely cosmetic. He drives this home later in the BlackRock appearance, declaring that the ultimate goal is to make AI “too cheap to meter.” “We want to flood the world with intelligence,” Altman said, and “we want people to just use it for everything.” But without addressing the capital-labor relationship he so astutely acknowledges, the question remains: who benefits when the flood comes for us all? More on Sam Altman: Humongous Numbers of People Are Uninstalling ChatGPT as Anti-OpenAI Sentiment Surges I’m a tech and labor correspondent for Futurism, where my beat includes the role of emerging technologies in governance, surveillance, and labor. Title: GTC preview: Inside the AI factory — The $1T infrastructure war under the hood of the AI economy
URL: https://siliconangle.com/2026/03/14/gtc-preview-inside-ai-factory-1t-infrastructure-war-hood-ai-economy/
Time Published: 2026-03-15T03:01:34Z
Full Content:
UPDATED 23:01 EDT / MARCH 14 2026 by John Furrier Every year the artificial intelligence industry gathers at Nvidia Corp.’s GPU Technology Conference expecting to see faster graphics processing units, bigger models and the next wave of AI software innovation. And yes, that will all be there. But if you’ve been watching the conversations we’ve been having on theCUBE over the past few years, you know the real story isn’t just happening on stage — it’s happening under the hood. What’s unfolding right now is the largest infrastructure buildout the tech industry has seen since the birth of the cloud. But unlike the cloud era, which was largely a software revolution, the AI era is rapidly becoming something else entirely. It’s becoming industrial. The companies racing to lead this new era aren’t just writing code. They’re securing power, booking semiconductor capacity, locking in memory supply and deploying massive clusters designed to produce intelligence at scale. We call this system the AI factory. And as we head into GTC, the deeper narrative isn’t just about the next GPU architecture. It’s about a global infrastructure race — a trillion-dollar supply chain trench war — to build the factories that will manufacture intelligence for the next decade. What’s even more interesting is that this factory is no longer confined to hyperscale data centers. It’s beginning to extend outward into what we call the hyperconverged edge, where AI moves closer to where data is created and decisions are made. That shift — from centralized AI to a distributed network of intelligence factories — may ultimately define the next phase of the industry. Over the past few years we’ve been describing a shift in how computing infrastructure is designed. Traditional data centers stored data and ran applications. AI infrastructure does something fundamentally different: It manufactures intelligence. An AI factory is a vertically integrated system designed to convert raw inputs — power, silicon, memory and data — into outputs such as AI models, inference services, automation and reasoning systems. Companies leading this transformation include Nvidia, Amazon.com Inc., Microsoft Corp., Google LLC, Meta Platforms Inc., among others. Collectively, these firms are investing hundreds of billions of dollars in AI infrastructure. Some of our reported estimates suggest the next phase of this cycle could approach $1 trillion in capital investment. GTC will showcase the latest pieces of this architecture — but the most important story lies deeper in the supply chain. One of the strangest economic dynamics in this cycle is what I call the GPU appreciation paradox. In traditional technology markets, hardware depreciates rapidly. But in the AI era, the opposite appears to be happening. Take the widely deployed Nvidia H100 GPU. Instead of losing economic value over time, the productivity of these chips is increasing as the models they serve become more powerful. As frontier models improve, the value generated by the compute serving those models rises. This is why some AI labs are locking in multiyear GPU contracts at roughly $2.40 per hour, well above estimated build costs. AI compute has become the most constrained resource in the digital economy. And that’s turning GPUs into something closer to productive capital assets than traditional information technoogy hardware. GPUs may get the headlines at GTC, but the real bottleneck in the AI factory could be memory. Modern AI systems rely heavily on long-context reasoning, meaning models can process huge sequences of text, code and multimodal data. That capability requires massive amounts of high-bandwidth memory or HBM. HBM is physically expensive: Our data suggest that by 2026, as much as 30% of hyperscaler capital expenditures could go toward memory alone (drumroll: Wait for all the price increases for systems). This shift is already reshaping semiconductor allocation across the industry, with memory supply increasingly prioritized for AI infrastructure. One of the key constraints we’re watching closely is semiconductor fabrication capacity. Datacenter accelerators are now consuming a growing share of manufacturing capacity at TSMC. Suppliers like NVIDIA (GPUs), Broadcom (TPUs and custom AI silicon), and others are committing to far more aggressive volume growth than traditional consumer chip designers. At a high level, there are two major constraints in the semiconductor manufacturing process that matter for AI infrastructure: 1. Front-end capacity: the upstream wafer fabrication stage, where advanced logic process nodes produce the silicon and logic circuits on wafers. 2. Back-end capacity: sometimes referred to as the mid-end where advanced packaging technologies such as CoWoS (Chip-on-Wafer-on-Substrate) integrate the chips with high-bandwidth memory (HBM), substrates, and other components to create the final accelerator packages. Manufacturers like TSMC have to carefully balance both stages of the process. Last year the back-end packaging stage, especially CoWoS, was the biggest bottleneck. While still tight, the constraint is increasingly shifting toward the front-end wafer fabrication capacity. The bottom line: AI demand is exploding, but silicon production is struggling to keep pace. To understand the ultimate limit of AI scaling, you have to go even further upstream. The real gatekeeper in the semiconductor ecosystem is ASML Holding NV, the Dutch manufacturer of extreme ultraviolet lithography machines. These tools are required to produce the most advanced chips. Each machine costs more than $350 million, contains hundreds of thousands of components, and relies on a supply chain of thousands of specialized suppliers. More importantly, production is limited. ASML can manufacture roughly 70 to 100 extreme ultraviolet tools per year through the end of the decade. That output effectively caps how quickly the world can expand advanced semiconductor production. You can innovate software at exponential speed. But you cannot easily scale the industrial manufacturing systems that produce the silicon. Another key input to the AI factory is energy. Frontier AI clusters now require enormous amounts of electricity — often measured in gigawatts. Yet power isn’t necessarily a hard scaling limit. It’s a cost problem. To bypass grid constraints and multi-year permitting timelines, hyperscalers and AI labs are increasingly deploying behind-the-meter power systems. These include natural gas turbines, modular microgrids, fuel cells and factory-built data center modules. Even if energy costs increase, the economics still favor early deployment because the marginal value of frontier AI models is so high. In the AI race, speed often matters more than efficiency. While the hyperscale AI factory is grabbing headlines, another important shift is happening further out in the infrastructure stack. The AI factory is expanding to the edge. Enterprises are increasingly deploying localized AI systems to support real-time decision-making in environments such as: factories, hospitals, retail stores, logistics centers and smart cities. These environments require low latency, data sovereignty and continuous operation even when cloud connectivity is limited. This is where the hyperconverged edge enters the picture. Hyperconverged edge platforms collapse networking, compute, storage, security and AI inference into unified edge infrastructure. Instead of isolated devices, organizations deploy distributed mini AI factories capable of running localized inference and synchronizing with centralized AI clusters. In this architecture, hyperscale AI factories train models, while hyperconverged edge systems operate those models in the real world. This distributed model of intelligence production will likely be a major theme emerging from GTC discussions. The AI infrastructure race is also becoming deeply geopolitical. Governments around the world increasingly see AI capability as a matter of national sovereignty. Control over AI infrastructure means control over economic competitiveness, industrial automation, defense systems and national innovation capacity The United States currently leads in several key areas, including AI software ecosystems and access to advanced manufacturing through partners such as Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. But other regions are moving quickly. China is pursuing full-stack vertical integration through companies such as Huawei, investing heavily in domestic chip design, memory production and sovereign AI infrastructure. Europe and Middle Eastern nations are also investing in sovereign compute capacity to ensure they are not dependent on foreign cloud providers. In many ways, AI factories are becoming the strategic infrastructure of the digital age, similar to energy grids or telecommunications networks. Perhaps the biggest misconception about the AI boom is how it is categorized. Many investors still treat AI companies as traditional software firms. But the companies leading the AI Factory buildout increasingly resemble heavy industrial operators. They are deploying gigawatt-scale data centers, global semiconductor supply chains, massive capital investment programs and vertically integrated infrastructure stacks This is not just another software cycle. It’s the emergence of industrial-scale intelligence production. As we head into another Nvidia GTC, it’s easy to get caught up in the product announcements like the next GPU, the next model, the next software framework. But the bigger story unfolding beneath the surface is about infrastructure power. The companies that win the next four to five years in AI won’t simply be the ones with the best algorithms. They’ll be the ones that secured the supply chain early: The ones that are locked in the lithography capacity. The ones that booked the memory wafers. The ones that built the power plants. The ones that extended their AI factories all the way out to the edge. Because the future of AI is no longer just about training bigger models. It’s about building the global system that runs them. The AI factory is quickly becoming the industrial backbone of the digital economy — a distributed network of hyperscale clusters and hyperconverged edge infrastructure that together produce and operate intelligence. And if the last decade belonged to the cloud, the next decade will belong to the companies that build and control these factories. That’s the story I’ll be watching closely this week at GTC. Because the race to build the AI factory is only just getting started. Support our mission to keep content open and free by engaging with theCUBE community. Title: JP Morgan shrinks lending to private credit companies
URL: https://observer.co.uk/news/business/article/jp-morgan-shrinks-lending-to-private-credit-companies
Time Published: 2026-03-15T00:00:00Z
Full Content:
Get full access to the app & website: Subscribe Business Sunday 15 March 2026 Finance Banking Share Related articles: Mark Carney to urge Starmer to join ‘world bank for defence’ The new tax regime has clear winners – but they’re not taxpayers JP Morgan, the world’s largest bank, has restricted lending to private credit firms after marking down the value of its loans to software companies. It’s a significant sign of distrust at a time of heightened volatility in global markets. Since the 2008 financial crisis, after which banks tightened lending criteria, private credit has grown fivefold into a $1.9tn market. But a string of high-profile bankruptcies in recent months has led investors to question whether private credit loans are worth less than advertised on the tin. That, in turn, has led them to pull more than $4.5bn in cash from leading private credit funds in the last month. Several managers, including Blackstone, BlackRock and Blue Owl, have set redemption limits in response, “gating” what can be withdrawn at one time. JP Morgan has fired a shot across the bow of an industry trying to muscle in on banks’ home turf of lending. But is this the start of a private credit downturn? Who will be exposed if liquidity dries up? As Warren Buffett said: “You only find out who is swimming naked when the tide goes out”. Initially, private credit industry funding came from large institutions, such as pension and sovereign wealth funds. But﻿, private credit firms found ways to bring in cash from individuals and retail investors via so-called “semi-liquid” funds. This flow has been increased by Donald Trump’s changes to 401(k) plans, which allow ordinary Americans to invest in alternative assets. Now many of those fleet-footed retail investors are heading for the exit – only to find those funds aren’t quite as liquid as they expected. “In many of these private markets there tends to be a ‘boom-bust’ cycle,” says Josh Lerner, professor at Harvard Business School. “Whether it’s buyouts before the [financial crash], or venture capital in 2021, or private credit now; it’s during these periods where you have a huge influx of capital that you tend to have a lowering of underwriting standards as a result.” The other worrying factor is AI. Software and technology companies accounted for roughly a quarter of private credit loans in 2025, according to S&P. The worry is that if AI agents can replace what those software companies do, revenues will collapse and loans won’t be repaid. UBS has warned that in an “aggressive disruption scenario”, default rates in US private credit could climb to 13%, up from a record 5.8% already. Last week, the Financial Times reported that a Goldman Sachs executive had let slip in an online seminar that his clients in private capital were “glad” the conflict in Iran was providing a “distraction” from questions over the sector’s exposure to software. But recent private credit-linked scandals will not fade easily. They include: the collapse of a little-known Mayfair-based mortgage provider MFS amid fraud allegations has left creditors nursing a potential £1.3bn potential shortfall against loans; and the unwinding of US subprime car lender Tricolor in September, despite receiving triple A ratings. Its executives are accused in court documents of manipulating data to make “near-worthless” assets appear to meet lenders’ requirements. Related articles: Mark Carney to urge Starmer to join ‘world bank for defence’ The new tax regime has clear winners – but they’re not taxpayers They deny the charges. Is this systemic? The key question is how deeply entangled private credit is with the banking system – and that includes UK institutions. Barclays, for instance, is already owed approximately £495m from its exposure to MFS and has made significant loans to private capital. Daily Sensemaker M T W T F S S Clear, calm analysis on the stories driving the day’s news. The Observer Daily M T W T F S S The very best of our journalism, reviews and ideas – curated each day. The Puzzle Edit M T W T F S S Puzzles people and the stories behind them - trends, histories, insights and brain teasers all in one place. View more For information about how The Observer protects your data, read our Privacy Policy “In 2008, the use of collateralised debt obligations and instruments like that meant the risk was hidden,” says Dr Jón Danielsson, director of the Systemic Risk centre at LSE. “Even if banks lend to private credit companies, there is only the arms length-type exposure. And importantly, it’s all visible.” But confidence matters. Private credit providers now face an unenviable choice: between blocking withdrawals and damaging trust or risking a fire sale of assets to meet redemptions. JP Morgan’s decision to move first may prove shrewd. Photograph by Getty Images Finance Banking

Title: The Washington Post Lost Over 60,000 Subscribers Following Winter Layoffs
URL: https://www.thewrap.com/media-platforms/journalism/the-washington-post-lost-60000-subscribers-layoffs-jeff-bezos/
Time Published: 2026-03-14T23:41:05Z
Full Content:
The New York Times also reports executive editor Matt Murray had plans to leave the newspaper ahead of the cuts The Washington Post lost over 60,000 subscribers following Jeff Bezos’ sweeping layoffs of 44% of the paper’s newsroom in February, The New York Times reported Saturday. The Times additionally detailed “how Bezos upended the Washington Post,” per its headline, including his decision to retain executive editor Matt Murray on staff to see through the layoffs, even after CEO Will Lewis’ departure. The Times also noted a “spokesperson for the Post disputed” the 60,000 figure, “but declined to provide an alternate number.” Murray had known the layoffs of 350 staffers in February were coming since at least November. Murray also told Lewis he was planning to leave, according to The Times. Bezos stepped in and presented a plan: “Reduce the newsroom’s budget by half and double the productivity of those who remained, all while protecting some core parts of The Post’s coverage, like investigative journalism. Mr. Murray said he would take on that challenge,” the Times reported. The story comes as Bezos is set to have met with the newspaper’s masthead and some reporters Thursday. The Amazon founder has made little public comment since his initial Feb. 7 statement following the layoffs when he announced Lewis would depart the organization. Jeff D’Onofrio, the outlet’s chief financial officer, was named interim CEO and publisher. At the time, Bezos said the Post “has an essential journalistic mission and an extraordinary opportunity.” He did not mention Lewis. “Each and every day our readers give us a roadmap to success. The data tells us what is valuable and where to focus,” Bezos added. “Jeff, along with Matt and Adam, are positioned to lead The Post into an exciting and thriving next chapter.” Murray described the layoffs as a “strategic reset” at the time.

Title: How I Shot That: The Cameras and Lenses Behind the 2026 SXSW Features
URL: https://www.indiewire.com/gallery/sxsw-2026-feature-film-camera-survey/
Time Published: 2026-03-14T19:30:20Z
Full Content:
By providing your information, you agree to our Terms of Use and our Privacy Policy. We use vendors that may also process your information to help provide our services. This site is protected by reCAPTCHA Enterprise and the Google Privacy Policy and Terms of Service apply. Throughout the year, IndieWire asks cinematographers with films premiering at major festivals to tell us what formats, cameras, and lenses they chose in order to create the look of their films, and how those choices helped them craft visual worlds that range from the gritty to the grandiose. The point is never to count the number of ALEXA 35s or Sony Venice 2s, nor to acknowledge how nice it would be to shoot on film if the budget allows. It’s to see how cinematographers get inventive, solve problems, and capture a protagonist’s inner-world or the feeling of being in a specific place and time through the imagery we see. If there’s a running theme among the narrative features playing at the 2026 SXSW festival, it’s scrappy cinematography. Sometimes this is quite literal. Danny Madden, the director and director of photography on “Downbeat,” told IndieWire that since the main conceit of his film involved making art with trash, it only made sense to shoot the movie with a Handycam. “It brings a kind of raw access that felt correct for this movie about a feral guy tumbling his way through a dirty city. Shooting on a camcorder is also sort of spiritually linked to the story — make with what you’ve got — and I had a $900 used camera I bought with Kickstarter money,” Madden said. Likewise, Gemma Doll-Grossman, the cinematographer of “Sender,” told IndieWire that the key element dictating the look of the film was cardboard boxes. “The camera remains stubbornly static (No pans! No tilts!), unless it’s rigged to an everyday object — a tube of lipstick, a pill bottle, a cardboard box — which offers the impression of surveillance,” Doll-Grossman said. “Most of the film is captured with 12mm and 18.5mm lenses, which emulated a security camera’s perspective and gave our protagonist a wide canvas to play. And, undoubtedly, our aspect ratio is boxy 3:2.” But scrappiness has a wide range of meanings. DP Bella Gonzales refused to be satisfied with the tropes of drug trip imagery for “Pizza Movie,” and spent months with her business partner Andrew Laboy researching and developing handmade custom filters. “We experimented with hundreds of materials — often layering and combining them — to create effects that felt original, tactile, and slightly surreal. To push those filters further, my camera assistants Michael “Moose” Belardi and Adam Gonzalez worked with gaffer Kevin Kim to build a custom matte box that allowed us to interact with the filters using external light. That flexibility gave us the ability to shape and activate the textures in camera, creating a visual language that felt playful, handcrafted, and specific to the film,” Gonzales told IndieWire. Read the answers below to see how each of these 50 cinematographers created a visual language specific to their film. Dir: John Valley, DP: Raylor Camarot Category: Midnighter Format: 6k Opengate X-OCN Camera: Sony Venice 1, FX3 Lenses: Zeiss Nano primes, Fujinon Premista 28-100 The Look: We tested a couple camera and lens options — at one point we even considered shooting super16 crop mode on an Alexa (almost polar opposite look). We ended up going with the Venice and Nano primes because they gave us a ‘medium format’ feel. Something about the square sensor and shallow depth of field resonated with us, allowing us to guide the audience’s attention without being heavy-handed visually. The Nano’s are sharp wide open, so even our wide shots have separation of focus. We chose 4:3 because we were shooting a lot of houses (both real and doll-sized), so a squarish aspect ratio just felt natural. Plus, I’ve always loved framing people in 4:3. There are a few tracking shots that we shot with an FX3 on a Ronin, only because that was what we could afford. We used the Premista zoom for very specific shots, but we only had it for the few days we needed it. Which was a blessing and a curse, John and I inevitably fall in love with a zoom every time we have one. John and I’s work has a distinct look, no matter how much we try and fight it. Eventually, instinct takes over, and our tastes align in a way that creates something slightly ‘off kilter’. It’s hard to put into words, but it’s inherently rough around the edges. Like the image is barely holding itself together. For this film, that tension was perfect. Dir & DP: Polaris Banks Category: Visions Format: 3.4K ARRIRAW Open Gate, 720p H.264, 1080p Full HD MPEG-4 Camera: ALEXA Mini, iPhone, Canon EOS 70D Lenses: Arri Master Primes, Angenieux Optimo DP Zooms, iPhone Lens (No Adapter) The Look: “And Her Body Was Never Found” is about a couple making a movie in the wilderness all by themselves. So we were restricted to static two shots and moving singles operated by each other, with absolutely no focus pulling. In spite of the limitations, we wanted to push what could be accomplished by only two people. So we bought a used ALEXA Mini and found a private owner willing to cut us a deal on some Master Primes, which would bring the wow factor just sitting on a tripod. We needed to carry the entire production on our backs for hours of hiking a day, though. So I limited our set to the 50mm, 35mm, and 18mm, which were still brutally heavy when added to the camera body and batteries. After two trips out, the rental and insurance were costing too much. So I had to switch to a pair of Angenieux Optimo DP zooms my brother owns to finish out the Alexa section. We also used a Tiffen softnet black 1 filter to take the digital edge off, but I probably should’ve gotten a pearlescent filter too, because you can see the net structure sometimes in the bokeh. Halfway through the narrative, we pivot to shooting on iPhones, which completely revitalized the up to that point grueling production. Mor reveled in adding little zooms and well-timed whip pans to punctuate the comedy and terror, really blurring the line between performance and cinematography. Instead of being locked into strict marks like the first leg of shooting, we chased each other all over the locations and let the autofocus do its thing. The freedom and joy really bleeds into those scenes. For the interview clip at the end, we rented five Canon 70Ds from five different owners off ShareGrid, which took us all day to pick up from every corner of LA. I had to pop up between each take, cut all five cameras, give notes, re-roll all five, then sit back down to act. It was a little silly, but having five angles was such a luxury to edit. Dir: Brian Tetsuro Ivie, DP: Matheus Bastos Category: Spotlight Format: 16mm Film Camera: Arriflex 416 Lenses: Cooke S4X + SK4 Primes, Cooke Varokinetal 10.4-52mm S16 Zoom The Look: “Anima” is a sci-fi road trip film that tells a cross-cultural and cross-generational story about a young woman embarking on a road trip with an old, wealthy businessman to preserve his consciousness at an experimental facility. We were equally inspired by both independent American and Japanese cinema from the ‘90s and early 2000s, but in a context of futuristic (or modern-day…) developments in technology, where that meets us and our humanity, and the ethics that lie in between. For that reason, we also wanted to give the film a handmade and textured look, but still have flavors of a classically shot film that you might’ve found on the shelf of a Blockbuster back in the day. We knew that the film was going to be shot on Kodak 16mm film for this very reason, and my mind immediately went to lensing the film with Cooke lenses for their warmth, clarity, and familiar look across the history of cinema. We specifically used the Arri Rental modified Cooke S4X (originally made for “The Last of Us” Season 2) because I loved having the modified round aperture and faster T-Stop in most of our lenses. For our wide-angle options, we used the Cooke SK4 lenses, which are specifically made for S16 capture. I also mixed in my personal Cooke Varokinetal 10.4-52mm S16 zoom lens, which feels like the perfect blend between a Cooke Speed Panchro and a Cooke S4. Anima was shot on 3 film stocks: 7203 50D, 7213 200T, and 7219 500T. The entire film was overexposed 1 stop for a clean and rich negative. We scanned with Kodak NYC in 4K on a Lasergraphics Scantation. Huge shout-out to director Brian Tetsuro Ivie for being the co-founder of KEBRADO, a celluloid-only production company developing, producing, and shooting projects exclusively on 16mm, 35mm, and 65mm film. Thank you to Arri Rental for the support on the film as well! Dir: Chelsea Devantez, DP: Veronica Bouza Category: Spotlight Format: 4.6K Prores 4444 Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Arri Signature Primes and Zooms The Look: “Basic” is a romantic comedy that dives into the insecurities in all of us. Visually, Director Chelsea Devantez and I wanted to create an elevated and modern look that paid homage to 2000s rom-coms. The ALEXA 35, paired with Arri Signature Primes and Zooms, gave us a beautiful balance of exceptional dynamic range and rich color rendition. For moments of heightened reality, we leaned more into a beauty look by glowing the highlights, adding a ½ Glimmer Glass. Dir: Will Ropp, DP: Matthew Pothier Category: Competition Format: Open gate ARRIRAW Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Panavision Primos and Ultra Speeds The Look: The lenses are not too out of the ordinary. There are some Panavision Ultra Speeds I love, so I got those, and I liked the primos as a base set since they didn’t call too much attention to themselves. We did stick to longer focal lengths for the most part to give it a slightly more isolated feel compared to a typical comedy shot on all wides. I didn’t want to rely too much on adding optical distortion to bring in texture, but to do it through lighting techniques. A big part of the look was not changing the white balance of the camera; we kept it set at 5600k as if it were a film stock and made all the adjustments with old-school color correction filters. We also only used tungsten lights as well to enhance that look. This was a technique I was experimenting with on some commercials that summer, and when we tested it during prep, it seemed to add some really interesting tonality to [Ben Wang’s] midtones and skin, so we went with it! Dir: Jonás Cuarón, DP: Pepe Ávila del Pino Category: Spotlight Format: ARRIRAW Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Mini Cooke s4, White Point, Canon zoom 8-64mm The Look: The sensor of the ALEXA 35 in combination with the optics of the Cooke, the white point, and the Canon zoom gave us the opportunity to render the colors and textures of the made-up world of Campeón. It also gave us the dynamic range and the information and detail needed for the work on VFX. Dir: Marq Evans, DP: Jason Roark Category: Spotlight Format: 4K, 6K Braw 16:9 Camera: Blackmagic P6k Pro, Blackmagic P4k, Canon 814 Super 8mm Lenses: Veydra Mini Cinema Primes The Look: Production for “Capturing Bigfoot” was an interesting mix of scheduled, 3-camera interviews and a lot of “catch what you can” verité. We often followed our colorful characters into unknown circumstances, and the small format of my Blackmagic cameras was key to being able to move quickly and shoot high-quality images from multiple angles. We’d often have one camera on sticks or a monopod, and another on a gimbal. I had great support from Erik Wallin and Gregg Frost when shooting different angles, and Erik shot some absolutely beautiful drone footage. Early in production, Director Marq Evans and I decided to use the Blackmagic cameras that I own. I love to rent an Alexa when I can, but having the kit ready to go at a moment’s notice was valuable. Marq teaches Documentary Filmmaking at Olympic College in Washington State, and is a great editor, fluent in the nuts and bolts of production, and the Braw Codec is solid and easy to work with. I love the Blackmagic Cameras for their image quality and small form factor, and my Veydra Mini Primes on the Pocket 4ks are gorgeous — so great for interviews. The Blackmagic cameras are small enough to easily pack for travel (there was a lot), and work well on the Ronin. I shot some of our recreation sequences on my trusty Canon Auto Zoom 814 Super 8. I’ve had it since college (found in a thrift store for $12 if I remember right!) and always love to get it in the mix. For recreations from the 1960’s, it felt perfect. Dir: Edd Benda & Stephen Helstad, DP: Cristina Dunlap Category: Spotlight Format: ProRes 4444XQ Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Konica Hexanons, Angenieux Optimo 24-290 The Look: Stephen, Edd, and I were fortunate to do extensive lens testing with one of our actors before heading out to shoot in Illinois. From the outset, we knew we wanted to work with the ALEXA 35, taking advantage of its low-light sensor, as we would be shooting in rural Illinois — an area known for being a “dark zone” with little to no ambient light at night. The ALEXA 35 allowed us to hold detail deep into the shadows while preserving highlight information, giving us the flexibility to let darkness exist naturally in the frame without sacrificing texture. After evaluating how each lens set rendered color, the Konica Hexanons became the unanimous choice. We were drawn to their cooler tonal quality to counterbalance the heavy warmth of our humid Midwestern summer locations — the kind of climate that naturally amplifies warm hues, especially in skin tones. We also knew we’d be staging a large practical fire in a pivotal scene and wanted to avoid an image that felt washed out or overwhelmingly warm. The Konicas are vivid, punchy lenses with strong natural contrast and beautifully vibrant greens and blues. They proved to be the perfect complement to both our locations and our evolving visual language. Part thriller, part dark comedy, we wanted the look of the film to shift as chaos descends upon the characters. It begins as a softer family dramedy and gradually transforms into something far more stark and high-contrast — mirroring the internal unraveling of our protagonists. We also incorporated the Angénieux Optimo 24–290 to convey moments when the world feels as though it’s closing in on Jess, our main character. The lens allowed us to subtly compress space and intensify that psychological pressure without disrupting the film’s visual continuity. Dir: Dempsey Bryk, DP: Kristofer Bonnell Category: Spotlight Format: ARRIRAW 4.6K Open Gate Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Zeiss Super Speed / Standard Speed The Look: When thinking about what lens and camera package to use for this film, Dempsey Bryk and I both knew early on that a lot of it would be shot handheld, and the takes would be quite long at times. Our locations were beautiful, but they carried a sense of wear and a subtle brokenness to them. After testing a handful of different lenses, we landed on the Super Speeds. Not only because they are small and fast, but because we also were looking for lenses that had a nice balance of vintage characteristics while still remaining sharp enough wide open when needed in low light. The lower contrast and delicate fall-off helped match the emotional tone of the film, embracing both the beauty and imperfection of the spaces. When pairing them with the standard speeds, it gave us a great range of focal lengths, giving us ultimate creative flexibility. The other lens in our kit, which we used for a number of scenes, was the 25-250 Angenieux HR. Having the zoom in the kit allowed us to take a step back and create an alternate perspective when we wanted to watch from an objective point of view, create distance between the characters, or add any immersive zooms. Shooting on the Alexa 35 was an easy choice. Aside from the unmatched colour science, beautifully accurate skin tones, and film-like texture, we had a lot of high noon sun and night scenes to contend with. I knew I was going to need the strong dynamic range of the camera on both ends of the scale. As we watched back dailies each day and later entered the post-production phase, it proved to be the right choice. Dir: Jo Rochelle, DP: Anup Kulkarni Category: Spotlight Format: 4K Ultra HD, Log C, QuickTime, Apple ProRes 4444, 2.39:1 aspect ratio Camera: ALEXA Mini, ALEXA Mini LF Lenses: ZEISS Compact Prime- CP.3 The Look: For “Dead Deer High,” the cinematography had to serve vulnerability above all else. This is a story about young poets navigating grief and rediscovering their voices. The camera needed to feel present and intimate, but never intrusive. It had to honor performance, hold space for silence, and respond truthfully to natural light. The ALEXA Mini LF was the right tool because it gave us both emotional depth and physical flexibility. The large-format sensor produces a subtle dimensionality that makes faces feel sculptural and grounded in space. In a film centered on spoken word, micro-expressions matter. The way the sensor renders skin tone, highlight roll-off, and color separation allowed us to capture those nuances without exaggeration. We often worked in available light or lightly shaped practical environments, and the camera’s dynamic range let us embrace shadow and softness without introducing noise or losing tonal integrity. Equally important was the camera’s compact, modular design. Much of the film takes place in real classrooms and rehearsal spaces. I needed to move fluidly in tight environments and operate close to the actors to preserve immediacy. I also built custom lens attachments for several experimental POV sequences. The lightweight body allowed us to execute those moments without technical constraints, keeping the emotional continuity intact. I paired the camera with Zeiss CP.3 primes because of their balance between clarity and character. The CP.3 lenses are sharp, but not sterile. They offer controlled contrast and a subtle organic texture that supported the film’s grounded tone. Their consistency across focal lengths helped maintain visual cohesion as we shifted from ensemble classroom scenes to intimate close-ups during performances. Together, this combination gave us a visual language. The tools disappeared, allowing the poetry, the performances, and the healing journey to remain at the forefront. Dir: Richard E. Williams, DP: Julian Panetta Category: Visions Format: 6k OCN XT Camera: Sony Venice Rialto Lenses: Custom-tuned Panavision H series 20mm The Look: The film’s unique, first-person perspective dictated a system that was not only lightweight but also as physically short as possible. The shortness is important for any hand acting from our operator, as well as getting the other characters to really get in the audience’s face when we needed to. The Rialto gave us all the usability and expected quality of a cinema camera, but in the form of a tiny body. We used a helmet rig, which ended up being surprisingly ergonomic and agile, however we put the Venice body on a backpack mount, which was worn by another crew member, so there was an added challenge of choreographing another crew member to stay close to the operator and stay out of shot, too. Richard loves the K35’s, but from an efficiency standpoint, why rent the whole set if you’re only going to use one lens? That’s part of why I love going to Panavision, you only need to hire what you’re actually going to use. Being able to have our optics custom-tuned meant we could have something that met the needs of the job, like great minimum focus and a small footprint with a lot of field curvature, halation, flares, and veiling glare, which directly reinforces the instability of Sean’s perspective. Its very easy to get caught up in figuring out what is a “realistic” field of view for humans, and truth be told its certainly a lot wider than a 20mm lens, but opting for something in that range allows us to be a lot more selective about what’s in and out of the frame, and also doesn’t overly distort close ups. All of this combined destabilises the image enough to keep it feeling subjective, not like Meta Glasses, which are just recording what’s in front of them. Dir & DP: Danny Madden Category: Spotlight Format: HD, XAVC Camera: Sony Handycam AX-100 Lenses: That little Zeiss zoomer built into the camera The Look: The joke was: This is a movie about a guy making art with trash, let’s do the same with the filmmaking. In truth, though, I love the look of the Handycam. It brings a kind of raw access that felt correct for this movie about a feral guy tumbling his way through a dirty city. Shooting on a camcorder is also sort of spiritually linked to the story — make with what you’ve got — and I had a $900 used camera I bought with Kickstarter money. This was my first film as cinematographer, but most of the time I operate the camera when I direct. On other films, even with an Alexa Mini stripped down as much as we could manage, I never felt that I could get at the agility I wanted, the kind of freedom of movement I had shooting as a teenager on a Canon GL1. That top-grip skate video kind of motion, lightness, and swiftness of process. This camera did it, and even though it shoots H264 we made it work on the back end. A shout-out to the film’s beloved colorist Sean Wells at Roast n’ Post in Oakland! As I sent him camera tests in pre-production, he was very supportive of the look of it, limitations and all. “Just get it as close as you can on the day,” he told me. That was excellent advice and a confidence-giving endorsement. Dirs: Raviv Ullman & Greg Yagolnitzer, DP: Ben Goodman Category: Midnighter Format: X-OCN ST (8.6K) Camera: Sony Venice 2 Lenses: ARRI Signature Primes and Zooms The Look: “Drag” unfolds over the course of a single night in one location. The central challenge in developing the look of the film was introducing visual variety without hindering cohesiveness or continuity. From early conversations with writers/directors Raviv Ullman and Greg Yagolnitzer, I knew the imagery would be bold. Some scenes take place in unexpectedly vibrant rooms filled with saturated artwork and expressive production design. Others occur in near-total darkness, where we avoided artificial “moonlight” through windows and instead sought to evoke the sensation of one’s eyes adapting to low-level ambience. To meet these and other demands, I chose to work with the Sony VENICE 2. I shot the entire film at its higher base ISO of 3200 with a 1-stop pulldown LUT, rating my meter at 1600 EI. This approach gave us substantial latitude in grading the film’s darkest scenes without introducing unwanted noise. It also allowed me to maintain a working stop of around T2.8 while lighting large background areas with a relatively modest package. The 8.6K resolution offered valuable compositional flexibility. I shot in the 17:9 mode framed for a 2:1 extraction with 5% safety on all sides, which gave us plenty of room to refine framing and occasionally stabilize shots in post. With generous support from ARRI Rental, we carried two complete camera packages: One permanently built for Steadicam and the other with the Rialto Extension System. The film employs graphic, unconventional perspectives — often in confined spaces — and our schedule required rapid transitions between handheld, dolly, crane, low-angle prism, and even a tree mount, among other configurations. The Rialto introduced versatility and efficiency without sacrificing image quality. I paired the VENICE 2 with ARRI Signature Primes, which I find strike a balance of technical precision and pleasing character. Their consistency across the full range of focal lengths unified the frames of the film. And critically, the wider lenses didn’t introduce distracting or unfavorable distortion. I must also say that while these tools were essential in making “Drag,” none of it would have been possible without our exceptional crew across all departments. Dir: Alex Prager, DP: Lol Crawley Category: Spotlight Camera: Sony Venice Lenses: Baltar The Look: An accomplished photographer asking a cinematographer to shoot for them is a huge compliment, but potentially a unique challenge in that you have two image makers on set. Alex made it very clear from the outset that she was looking for a collaborator, not just a mimic for her own aesthetic. Recognising that the majority of my work was grounded in a more naturalistic and often ‘caught’ aesthetic she joked that I could bring the spirit of Robert Altman to her appreciation of Hitchcock and Douglas Sirk. Alex had already spent weeks drawing her own storyboards which was vital in terms of conveying her intended coverage and visual language for each scene but she was also entirely willing to build on these ideas and we had a wonderful time shot listing at her studio, acting out the scenes together and honing the visual language of the film. I have shot many feature films on 35mm, including the VistaVision camera system, and Alex shares my love for the celluloid aesthetic. Unfortunately, it was not possible to shoot “DreamQuil” on film (aside from shooting the miniatures in VistaVision), and so in collaboration with Picture Shop LA, we started to explore the ways in which we could make our Sony Venice digital camera closely emulate the look of 35mm. Our final DI colorist, Tim Stipan, along with his Picture Shop colleagues, worked tirelessly to create our show LUT, emphasizing the saturation of the reds and other key colours integral to the production, costume, and makeup design, whilst keeping the remaining colour palette a soft pastel. After thorough testing, we chose to shoot on a combination of older Baltar and Kowa prime lenses combined with A, B & C Mitchell diffusion filters to reduce the ultra-sharp definition and detail level of modern cinema cameras. We used Mole-Richardson lights to bring yet a further classical cinema feel. Dirs: Megan Rico and Kade Atwood, DP: Brenna Empey Category: Competition Format: XAVC-I 4K Camera: Sony FX6 Lenses: Tokina Zooms The Look: Our approach to “Edie Arnold’s” cinematography was a loving embrace of scrappiness and speed. Principal photography was ten days with very limited resources and budget, which meant I sought out a camera that I’d normally bring onto a documentary project — the Sony FX6. Its versatility, featherweight body, and stellar performance in low light (dual ISO till I die!) fit our needs to a T. These limitations also forced us to get hyper-strategic with our shot list. It was all about moving quickly without sacrificing style or comedic timing, which resulted in directors Kade and Megan and I designing many locked-off, scene-encompassing tableaus for the characters to romp around in, in lieu of relying on traditional coverage, which would’ve put too much pressure on our already challenging schedule. I think our choreographed wides really shine because of this planning. Some days, there was little-to-no camera operating on my end — the directors and I talked more than once about this approach, making some shots “painterly”. And when you’re only given ten days to shoot a feature, it’s time to break out not only the Sachtler Flowtech sticks but also a set of zooms, in our case, the Tokina 25-75mm and 50-135mm. They lean cool-toned, which I find gorgeous. Typically, I’ll reach for a set with a wider stop, but the physical and visual comedy in this film meant opening up beyond a T/2.8 (and thereby letting the background fall into mush) never quite served the jokes we were trying to tell. We rarely ventured wider than an T/4. Yes, my 1st AC was in heaven. Dirs: Kevin Bacon and Kyra Sedgwick, DP: Mac Fisken Category: Spotlight Format: Prores 4k Camera: DJI Ronin 4D-8k Lenses: Sirui Saturn 1.6x Anamorphic The Look: When Kyra and Kevin first described this project with so many characters, animals, spontaneity, and a lot of movement, I immediately felt like the 4D would be a great fit. It allowed us to work fast and flexibly, running around the large environment without a big footprint but still moving with the actors in a way that felt very intimate. When Phil the goat moved the wrong way, we could adjust on the fly and get a push in that captured the moment and still felt cinematic. We shot everything on location, so the camera’s ability to jam into a corner of a tiny bathroom or pass through a window meant we could get shots that I’ve never been able to get before. Ultimately, it was a tool that freed us up to play with the shots — crucial for a movie about the joy of indie filmmaking. Dir: Meredith Alloway, DP: Karim Hussain Category: Spotlight Format: 4K ProRes 4444 XQ, LOG C4, Arri Texture G733 Nostalgic Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Canon K-35, Canon FD, Angenieux 25-250 Original Vintage Zoom The Look: “Forbidden Fruits” needed a softer patina to the image, a dreamy and 1960s/1970s Giallo vibe to counter the crass modern cleanliness of a functioning mall. The women had to look iconic. We didn’t want to use excessively heavy diffusion that would make the practicals bloom too much, so we chose Canon K-35 primes and shot them all wide open, which gave a very magical glow to the images naturally. For a zoom, we used a 1970 original Angenieux 25-250 zoom nicknamed “Lucky Pierre,” which has fungus in his rear element, but really adds a unique feeling to images because of that. We shot with the Arri Alexa 35 camera, baking in the G733 Nostalgic texture into the image, which gave a beautiful, painterly, and extremely filmic texture to everything. All shots were done at 2000 ISO to organically create the grain, as the Alexa 35 reacts to light in a similar manner as film stock, only with greater latitude. I used the Cinefade on every shot as a rapid remote variable ND, to swiftly balance both A+B cameras, while keeping the different speed lenses on them all wide open. I have used this technique on multiple projects before, and it’s always been a great time saver. It can have a slight green cast that you can dial out if desired, but you have to use it precisely to not get vignetting on the edges of the frame if you go beyond ND1.8. The color scheme of the movie and lighting was meticulously planned out in advance, following the narrative flow, starting warmer then progressively getting colder in terms of color. This was done in camera using color temperature settings, then augmented in the final color grade with Jim Fleming at Picture Shop. The lighting oscillates between an adapted version of the bright, practical mall lighting that we tweaked, to much more shadowy, stylish light during the Paradise rituals and the climax. Shots done through two-way mirrors also added infinity reflection effects for the changing room confessionals, to illustrate the multi-layers of emotions these women are feeling. Dir: Dario Russo, DP: Matthew Chuang Category: Spotlight Format: 4.6K Arriraw Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Panavision Primo primes and zooms The Look: Director Dario Russo wanted a very ’70s approach to the visual style with no moving camera and very minimal coverage. We chose to go with the ALEXA 35 and primarily the Primo 27mm as it framed our actors within the space in a way that felt right to us. Part of the ’70s feel we captured scenes with only a slow zoom. The Primo 19-90mm gave us the range we needed for those moments. Dirs: Ed Dougherty and Brea Grant, DPs: Alexander Chinnici, Mark Evans, Yaron Levy, Taylor Washington Category: Midnighter Format: 2.8K ARRIRAW Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: K 35’s for Content Moderation and Optimo Zooms for Union Meeting The Look: We chose the K35’s for their color rendering and softness. “Content Moderation” is all about building the tension and world of the pit as the main character is driven mad, and these lenses were perfect for giving us our unique looks. (I was also inspired by their use on “Aliens”) The Optimos were perfect for our budget and “Succession”-esque filming style while keeping us quick on our feet to get the coverage Ed needed for the union meeting. We wanted each segment to feel very different, so we aimed to use varying lenses and visual language. For “Content Moderation,” we utilized Sticks/fluid movement on primes to portray the claustrophobia and madness of the world, and for “Union,” we stuck with handheld with zooms for the fun chaos/action of our ensemble cast. — Taylor Washington Dir: Drew Kirsch, DP: Brett Pawlak Category: Spotlight Format: 4K Pro Res 4444 Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: K-35 ‘s The Look: For “He Bled Neon,” we chose the ALEXA 35 paired with vintage K-35 lenses, a combination that gave us the flexibility of a modern digital camera while retaining some of the character of older glass. The story unfolds across two aspect ratios that mirror the emotional arc of the character. When the frame expands into the taller 1.43 format, we utilized the full Super 35 sensor, allowing the image to feel more immersive while giving us the latitude to hold detail in both highlights and color. Lighting was approached in a very practical way. Because of the camera’s sensitivity, I relied heavily on real practical fixtures to motivate and often provide the majority of the light in the scene. Working closely with production designer Carlos Lazlo, we designed many of these practicals directly into the sets so that the lighting could live naturally within the world of the film. It became a true collaboration between cinematography and production design, where lamps, fixtures, and architectural elements were doing as much storytelling as traditional film lights. Rather than leaning on lens artifacts like heavy flares or distortion, the visual identity of the film comes more from color and contrast. Together with colorist Ian Vertovec at Light Iron, we developed a custom LUT loosely inspired by Ektachrome. In the final grade, the image pushes toward bold color and aggressive contrast, with a texture that feels closer to a 35mm point-and-shoot photograph — slightly imperfect, tactile, and grounded in the world of the story. Dir: Caleb Phillips, DP: Allie Schultz Category: Midnighter Format: 4K ProRes 4444 Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Cooke Panchro Classics The Look: “Imposters” is a mystery thriller that centers on the crumbling relationship of a couple after their son goes missing. The director, Caleb, and I opted for a naturalistic visual approach, grounding this story in the real world as much as possible. From early on, we both gravitated towards shooting spherical and cropping to a 2.39 aspect ratio. There’s a sense of realism and lack of artifice that comes with shooting spherical that was perfect for capturing the truth of these characters. We tested a variety of lenses and found the Cooke Panchro Classics were everything we were looking for. They fall into a sort of Goldilocks zone for softness and sharpness. There’s clarity, but they’re not so sharp that it looks clinical, and at wide open, focus falls off around the edges, giving dimensionality to the image in a really beautiful way. Also, I love that with these lenses you get the optical characteristics of a vintage lens, but with the mechanical benefits of a modern housing. Our schedule required us to move fast, and these lenses were super reliable to work with. I knew from the beginning that one of our biggest challenges would be shooting at night in the woods. The woods are a major character in this story, and the low light sensitivity of the ALEXA 35 was essential. For certain scenes, we wanted to light with mostly practical flashlights and lanterns that the actors would be carrying. I heavily utilized the enhanced sensitivity mode of the ALEXA 35, along with the deep shadow texture, to capture these scenes with minimal lighting. The incredible dynamic range of the ALEXA 35 made it possible for us to make our days amidst an intense schedule. Every second counted, and that meant sometimes there wasn’t enough time to net a window or control a highlight, but I never had to worry about the exposure clipping since I knew we had tons of flexibility in the image once we got to the color grade. Dir: Peter Warren, DP: Conor Murphy Category: Spotlight Format: 4.6K ProRes Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Zeiss Super Speeds The Look: “Kill Me” is a mystery film, and Peter and I wanted the audience to discover that they could trust neither the characters nor the images we placed in front of them. We trained the camera alternatingly on the current timeline of the film, the fractured memories of the protagonist, and the evolving, competing theories that he entertains as he goes about his detective work. We considered a variety of visual tools to create clear distinctions between each of these perspectives, but in the end, we opted for only subtle color and lighting cues to set them apart and to instead leave room for uncertainty. We did use a distortion filter based on the deakinizer lens to depict a few especially immersive memories. We wanted to drown the audience in evidence and speculation, and the resulting daily shotlist was like nothing I had ever contended with before. While a laid-back shoot can be nice, the satisfaction of filming “Kill Me” was found instead in packing each day with Tetris-like efficiency and working our way through volumes of disembodied inserts without getting lost or snagged. The dense, fast-paced sequences that you see in the film are the result of substantial planning. We opted for Zeiss Super Speeds in order to shoot quickly in tight spaces, and the Alexa 35 for its range and filmic color space. We often used 4′ and 6′ sliders for short pushes, or to find shoulders and hug the line. There were times during the shoot that I found myself wishing for the conveniences of a stage, but when I watch the film, I can see that it thrives off of the claustrophobia that practical locations imposed on us. Dir: Arabella Oz, DP: Jeff Griecci Category: Competition Format: 4.6K 3:2 Open Gate, ProRes 4444 XQ Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: TLS Cooke Speed Panchro Primes & the Cooke Varotal Mkiii The Look: In my earliest conversations with Arabella and Nick for “Mallory’s Ghost,” they referenced classic comedies, both romantic and supernatural, spanning decades as far back as the 1940’s. I especially latched onto their inspirations from the 70’s, as I felt that era’s look best fit the overall tone of Arabella’s script. We leaned into that visual mindset. We knew that a filmic look over a modern digital glossiness would be the approach. We wanted a gentle vintage quality, something that would handle skin tones well and provide a generally warm feeling overall. It became obvious that the Cooke Speed Panchro primes would be the choice to pair with our Alexa 35 package. We added a Cooke 25-250mm Varotal Mkiii into the mix for our zoom shots. I remember on Day 1 of production, when we broke out the 100mm prime (which we almost passed on, including in the rental, before Arabella insisted). I saw just how remarkable the image looked when we opened up the lens for a portrait: a frame of the ghost character sitting in the garden, reflecting on her mortal self. The focus roll-off and the bokeh, with the lens wide open, was so striking. It gave just the right subtle amount of dreaminess we wanted to help distinguish the fantasy from reality. Shooting with rehoused primes from TLS and the Alexa 35 made the practicalities of the production a breeze. I think the vintage Cookes paired with that camera make for such a pleasing image. The combination of old glass and modern tech provides the best of both worlds; a really nice combination for indie productions. Dir: Nan Feix, DP: Matt Batchelor Category: Spotlight Format: Super 16mm Kodak Stock. 7219 500T and 7207 250D Camera: Arri 416 Lenses: Arri/Zeiss S16 Ultra Primes and Canon 8-64mm 2.4 The Look: For me, the approach to this film was all about simplifying things. Shooting a film that uses all live locations, almost all real people, and the unpredictability that comes with that requires you to be prepared for anything. Film was always part of the dogma and visual approach. The 416 is simply the best film camera ever made, in my opinion. There is so much life and texture in the neighborhoods of Chinatown, and 16mm was the format that best brought that life to the screen. The Ultra 16 lenses are predictable and sharp and allowed us to let the grit and texture come through the beautiful locations. Marni and the crew at Panavision NY were super helpful in making sure we had what we needed. We needed a format that allowed me to pull my own focus, operate in tight spaces, and react to what was happening around us. I couldn’t imagine using another format for this film. Our Director Nan Feix, Producer Marine Garnier, and Loveboat were all super supportive of shooting this film on celluloid.
