Dir: Chandler Levack, DP: Jeremy Cox Category: 24 Beats Per Second Format: ARRIRAW Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Panavision G-Series Anamorphics The Look: In “Mile End Kicks,” Grace moves from her hometown to Montreal for the summer. We used anamorphic lenses to accentuate the feeling of seeing and experiencing something for the first time. Dirs: Matt Black and Ryan Polly, DP: Philip Lozano Category: Midnighter Format: 2,39:1 aspect ratio (anamorphic 2X desqueeze), 4K 6:5 XOCN XT recording Camera: Sony Venice 1, iPhone 15 PRO, MacBook Pro, Lipa HDV, GoPro Hero4, Webcam HP 320 FHD, Samsung mobile, Baby cam, PC laptop webcam, Sony nightvision handycam, Panasonic handycam, DJI Inspire 2. Lenses: Panavision T serie: 28/35/40/50/65/75/100/150. Panavision 90mm super macro. Panavision zoom 42-425 ALS10 The Look: With Matt & Ryan, we wanted the film to have a modern look, but personally, I hate modern sharp lenses. The Panavision T series and Sony Venice were the perfect combo — modern, with very few aberrations and with a subtle softness, giving a certain cinematic look, “out of reality” with the use of anamorphic lenses at the same time. We really wanted to embrace different looks shooting through many “cameras”, to show that the “disease” had spread over all devices and contents, as a metaphor for what happens in real life when people get sucked into scrolling and watching dumb content rather than connecting with other people. I liked that idea and really wanted to embrace it as much as we could, shooting with many devices/cameras/”shitty cams,” to give a certain visual richness through “rough looks” in addition to the elegance of our combo Sony Venice/T series. The cinematography tended to be natural but always stylized, and in such a horror genre movie I love to use lighting to show characters, or not — often silhouetting them, to narratively rise the level of fear of the audience, showing more or less of what they can or could see, or not, and always trying to bring depth and richness to the image. Dir: Dave Boyle, DP: Patrick Ouziel Category: Midnighter Format: 8.2K X-OCN LT and 4K XAVC S-I Camera: Sony Venice 2 and FX3 Lenses: Sigma FF High Speed Primes and Fujinon Cabrio 19-90mm The Look: In our early discussions, director Dave Boyle referenced films like “Don’t Look Now” (1973) and “The Changeling” (1980), and we were both interested in exploring a bit of a retro aesthetic. In practice, this meant embracing zooms, harder and less naturalistic lighting, as well as leaning into heavier haze use than I was accustomed to. My lighting director, Hidenori Nagata, was a master of using small mirrors to add interesting shapes all over the set. The mirrors also had a beautiful falloff when lighting faces. Our main camera package was a Venice 2, Sigma FF High Speed Primes, and a Fujinon Cabrio 19-90mm. We mostly used the 24mm, 35mm, and 50mm, because we like the feeling of presence you get when the camera is closer to the actor. The haze on set softened the contrast of our lenses and also added halation to windows and bright highlights. To punctuate certain psychologically intense moments for our main character, we used slow motion as well as a Laowa 24mm Pro2be, which allowed us to shoot from a unique perspective. The FX3 was used sparingly, mostly when the Venice was too big and heavy. We rigged it to a door and also sprinted with it for a “Raimi-cam” shot. I was so excited to work with Dave because he and I have very similar sensibilities when it comes to blocking and camera movement. Dave often describes our approach as “stately,” by which he means controlled dolly moves (sometimes combined with zooms) and elegant blocking that allows shots to develop in ways that engage an audience. For that reason, our Chapman Peewee III Plus (operated by my amazing dolly grip, Tsuneyuki Aiko) was also crucial. Camera placement is everything, and being able to quickly find exactly the right camera height for a shot is essential (not to mention being able to change it during a shot). Also, because I really favor Coen brothers-style top-down inserts, having a dolly makes these easily achievable. If I’m forced to pick between a more expensive camera and a dolly, the dolly wins every time. Dir: Ben Wheatley, DP: Armando Salas, ASC Category: Spotlight Format: REDCODE RAW Camera: Red Raptor XL[X] Lenses: Tribe7 Primes and Arri Signature Zooms The Look: Early in prep, director Ben Wheatley asked what an edgy version of “High Noon” shot in the 1970s during a blizzard might look like. That concept became the foundation of our visual approach. We created a film-emulation LUT with deep shadows, sharp contrast, and noticeable grain, paired with gentler glass to add subtle texture and personality — we wanted the image to feel tactile and weather-beaten. The softness of winter light, combined with atmospheric haze, allowed the shadows to open up in a natural way while still maintaining the grit we were after. The RED V-Raptor [X] was hugely beneficial for this approach because of its exceptional low-light sensitivity and color rendition. The global shutter was also key — it allowed us to capture strobing police lights and muzzle flashes in the middle of a blizzard without the distracting artifacts or broken frames that occur with rolling-shutter cameras. Dir: Jorma Taccone, DP: Matt Weston Category: Headliner Format: ARRIRAW Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Hawk Class-X The Look: It’s not every day you get the opportunity to remake your own work. The whole experience was fascinating: Being able to watch the same story be brought to life by a whole different cast, in new locations, from a different director’s perspective. Again: Fascinating. Probably not unlike a sequel, we had a blueprint. The original (on which we used Mini LFs with Master anamorphics). A starting point from which we could ask ourselves what we liked and wanted to keep, and what could be improved upon. When selecting gear for a project, I think I’m always looking for a balance between character and practicality, within the bounds of the budget and what’s available to us. Arri cameras are always a solid foundation. I know what I’m able to get out of them. Easy to use. Reliable. They just stay out of the way and get the job done, allowing us to focus our energy on the rest of the process. The Hawks, I hadn’t had experience with before this. After a little testing and comparing, they just felt right. Hitting that balance. Interesting, but not distracting. I suppose we approached shooting the movie in much the same way. Finding the most interesting ways to tell the story without overcomplicating things and distracting from it. Dir: Millicent Hailes, DP: Ksusha Genenfeld Category: Visions Format: 4.6K ARRIRAW Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Zeiss High Speeds and Angenieux Optimo 24-290 The Look: When I was first approached to shoot “Perfect,” our director Millicent Hailes wanted the world to feel raw, gritty, real, and textured. We both love the look of film and wanted the digital image to feel as close to that as possible. After testing a few options, we landed on the Zeiss High Speeds. Paired with the Alexa 35, these lenses gave us a slightly distorted yet clean image that felt grounded and real while still carrying a vintage quality. We wanted the lighting to feel very natural, almost effortless, but also dry and hot. The world we’re showing isn’t very comfortable to exist in. It’s summer, it’s sweaty, resources are limited, and the characters are young, messy, and still figuring themselves out. We wanted to lean into that unpolished feeling. Most of the film was shot handheld, which kept the camera feeling alive, and the compact size of the lenses made that approach possible. We also had many scenes that took place during blue hour, so having lenses that open up to T1.3 allowed us to keep shooting as long as possible as the light faded. These lenses were truly magical for us, and we couldn’t have done the project without the support of ARRI Rental. Millicent and I both love long takes that linger and build tension, so we occasionally used the 24–290 Optimo (thank you to my mentor Bill Bennett, ASC, for lending it to us). I’ve always been fascinated by shots where you don’t get to cut away, you stay with the actor, you live with them in the moment instead of cutting away. Some of our most emotional scenes work exactly like that: long zooms that slowly settle on the main character and allow the performance to breathe. This film felt like magic to make. It had the feeling of summer camp. We really got to make an honest movie, and mostly because I got to make it with my friends. Dir: Jake Kuhn and Noah Stratton-Twine, DP: Murray Zev Cohen Category: Visions Format: XAVC-I DCI 4K 422:HQ 10bit Camera: Sony FX6 Lenses: Sony 28-135mm F4 The Look: The Sony FX6 is a brilliant camera for smaller films like this. It’s more manageable for limited camera teams and multiple setups during the day. Shooting in 4k gave my director and editor the room to play with the footage through cropping where appropriate, and also having flexibility in the grade. This film was shot in 4:2:2 HQ 10-bit, which gave us very good information in the Raw files. Since this film was shot to be black-and-white, it was accompanied by a monochromatic LUT with a fairly high contrast. The camera was able to retain information in the shadows and highlights. I love the quick buttons on the FX6 and the customisable features. Anything to allow the day to flow better is always beneficial. The internal ND is fantastic for a camera with this price point, too. Paired with a circular polariser, it helped paint interesting images on the seashores of our locations. The single zoom lens was also ideal; the style of filmmaking for “Pincer Point” required several live zoom shots, so having the 28-135mm was a good, affordable option. A nicely timed zoom can really elevate tension and draw the audience in; finding that moment in each scene sometimes took a few takes. A zoom lens is also just more efficient for choosing a frame when under time constraints. However, this lens is fairly sharp, so I pretty much always place a BPM filter in front to take the edge off. The clarity this lens brings is still top-notch, so I find it quite reliable. Dir: Josh Plasse, DP: John Rosario Category: Spotlight Format: ARRIRAW 4.6K Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Legacy Anamorphic 1.8x The Look: “The Pirate King” follows Todd Gillis (Rob Riggle), a down-and-out ex-Marine wrestling with trauma who slowly finds rehabilitation through an unlikely community of pirate reenactors. Director Josh Plasse and I set out to build a world that felt textured, raw, and authentic, grounded in reality but open to the subtle visual liberties offered by reenactment and Todd’s emotional progression. The ALEXA 35 was an obvious choice for me. Its dynamic range, resolution, and color science are well known, but what draws me to it is something simpler, more poetic. It is a reliable canvas, one that receives each stroke of light faithfully and with precision. The ability to add texture to the digital negative aligns with my philosophy of building the look of the film in camera as much as possible. Over time, it has become the most consistent and sharpest knife in my kit. I know its edge, how it carves light, renders a moment, and how to let it do its quiet work. The Legacy 1.8x anamorphic lenses developed by Stephen Gelb of Lensworks became central to the film’s visual language. They offered a rare combination of qualities that felt right for this world. I wanted character and distortion without distraction, a gentle falloff to isolate Todd within the frame and emphasize his loneliness, and expressive flares that echo the theatrical spirit of the pirate reenactments. Their compact size and minimal focus distance allowed us to remain nimble inside Todd’s small sailboat and close to the actors without relying on diopters. Todd’s world begins bleak and desaturated, almost suspended. As he encounters the pirates, warmth enters the frame through richer saturation, gold hues, and a subtle lingering red that hints at danger. Green, symbolizing renewal and healing, became a recurring motif we called Seaside Green, a custom gel combination developed with my gaffer Anthony Stivale and supported by a bespoke LUT crafted with colorist Nick Hasson of THE END. The look was a collective act, shaped by our crew, our tools, and the textures of Virginia Beach. In the end, everyone and everything had a hand in painting the image. Dirs: Nick Kocher and Brian McElhaney, DP: Bella Gonzales Category: Spotlight Format: 4.6k ProRes Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Hawk V-Lite + Cooke Speed Panchros The Look: From the beginning, Nick and Brian were drawn to the texture and character of early-2000s comedies. While shooting on film was part of the early conversation, the realities of budget, schedule, and the fast-moving style they prefer on set made a digital workflow the more practical path. For that reason, I chose the ALEXA 35. One of the key considerations was avoiding the extremely shallow depth of field often associated with large-format sensors and instead maintaining an image closer to the feel of traditional 35mm photography. My goal was to shape a digital image that still carried a sense of photochemical texture. To help achieve that, we added grain in post and developed a custom LUT pipeline designed around a film-style tonal curve. We built the lighting strategy to feed into that curve so the image would respond with highlight roll-off and density reminiscent of film. Lens choice became the most important creative decision in achieving that visual language. Working closely with Keslow Camera, who are longtime collaborators of mine, I spent time testing what felt like nearly every lens option available. The film centers on a drug experience; it was important to clearly differentiate between the grounded reality of the story and the heightened perspective of the trip itself. That exploration led me to vintage spherical lenses, particularly the TCS-rehoused Cooke Speed Panchros, whose softer falloff and distinctive coatings paired beautifully with the Alexa 35. For the trip sequences, I leaned into something deliberately more “cinematic” in the most playful sense of the word. Filtration became another key part of that design. Rather than relying on conventional diffusion or post effects associated with drug imagery, my business partner, Andrew Laboy, and I spent months researching and developing handmade custom filters. We experimented with hundreds of materials — often layering and combining them — to create effects that felt original, tactile, and slightly surreal. To push those filters further, my camera assistants Michael “Moose” Belardi and Adam Gonzalez worked with gaffer Kevin Kim to build a custom matte box that allowed us to interact with the filters using external light. That flexibility gave us the ability to shape and activate the textures in camera, creating a visual language that felt playful, handcrafted, and specific to the film. Dir: Robb Boardman, DP: Markus Mentzer Category: Competition Format: 6K XOCN-LT Camera: Sony Burano Lenses: Sony G-Master Primes The Look: Simplicity and texture were the key principles that guided our choice of camera package. Robb and I knew early on that more than half the movie would be shot with a skeleton crew (without an AC). We also wanted the movie to be a love letter to LA, so capturing the city’s natural light was key. The Burano’s dynamic range is incredible, and its resolution and cutting-edge compression allowed us to bring back all that texture in post (thanks to Colorist Gabe Sanchez). Utilizing Sony G-Master Primes in conjunction with Black Satins and Hollywood Black Magic diffusion let us leverage Sony’s autofocus system, while also softening the appearance of lines and hard shadows. “Plantman” is an ensemble comedy inspired by thrillers like “Crimson Tide” and “Collateral,” where realism ultimately guides the story. Knowing that much of the movie would play in 2 and 3 shots, we found that a 2.20:1 aspect ratio gave us our widescreen feel, while still holding enough room for comedic performance. Dir: Vicky Jewson, DP: Bridger Nielson Category: Headliner Format: 4.6K ARRIRAW 2X Anamorphic Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Vantage Hawk Class-X The Look: “Pretty Lethal” is a trip down the rabbit hole into a physical manifestation of the lost dreams of Uma Thurman’s character, Devora. For me, it was a rich opportunity to blend the two sides of Eastern Europe. This idea led to combining different eras of practical light fixtures into the design of our main location, a mafia den in rural Hungary. Upstairs would have the opulent chandeliers, candelabras, and goose-neck sconces, and the lower floors would have the late 20th-century retrofit with mixed fluorescent, sodium, and mercury fixtures with visible conduit. I like to remain open to new lens and body combinations to best serve the story we are trying to tell. We always knew that we wanted a classic 2.39:1 scope delivery, and so we tested several sets of 2x squeeze lenses on 3 different camera bodies. We shot a local Hungarian ballet dancer under a bunch of mixed lighting scenarios using all possible camera /lens combos. I was expecting the Hawk vintage 74’s to be the choice, but the Class-X on the A35 sensor at 1600 gave us a combo that felt organic, but also fantastical with just the right amount of flare characteristics to set that off-kilter, fairy-tale mood. This camera, lens, and practical lighting combo also rendered significant nuance in color on varied skin tones and beautiful fall-off. It lets us create a visual journey of different color contrasts and combinations coming from the fixtures you see in the picture, which mimics the journey of the characters through the story. Vicky and I originally bonded over a single image of 5 ballerinas crammed in a booth in an Eastern European diner. That image became our north star for the green peeling walls and pink lights of the bar. The mix of colors, fixtures, and trims let the space speak so much about who all the characters are and also be a place that could turn from welcoming to threatening as the troupe has to escape from the bowels of the inn. Dirs: Tyler Gillett and Matt Bettinelli-Olpin, DP: Brett Jutkiewicz Category: Headliner Format: 4.6K ARRIRAW Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Cooke S5i The Look: As a sequel that begins immediately after the events of the first film, I wanted to keep the look consistent enough with the first film so that they could be watched together seamlessly, while still giving the sequel its own identity. I used the same lenses as the first film (Cooke S5i) but switched from the ALEXA Mini to the ALEXA 35, which I love for its exquisite color rendition, especially in skin tones, as well as its dynamic range and compact size. Shooting on spherical lenses allowed us to maintain close proximity to the actors and really put the audience within the action of the film, and the smaller camera body was great for all of the handheld and steadicam work we had throughout. My primary focus was to immerse the audience in Grace (Samara Weaving) and Faith’s (Kathryn Newton) journey, which often involved moving with the camera to underscore the intensity, tension, or suspense of each sequence. This film opens up the world from where the first ends, and the ALEXA 35 was great for handling all of our shooting environments from bright daylight to dark firelit rooms, and because I used a lot of mixed lighting to create color contrast and often to introduce a sense of unease to our interiors, it was important to have a camera that accurately captured the nuances of the lighting within the spaces. Dir: Gloria Mercer, DP: Devan Scott Category: Spotlight Format: 4K X-OCN, 6K Blackmagic Raw, and 4K X-AVC-HS Camera: Sony Venice (Super-35mm Mode), Blackmagic Pocket 6k (B-Cam), Sony A7IV (C/Crash Cam) Lenses: Fujinon Cabrio 19-90mm, Cooke S4 18/25/32/40/50/75/100mm, Super Takumar 50mm (W/ Tilt-Shift Adapter), Ironglass 58mm, Sigma 18-35mm, Sigma 24-70mm, Glimmerglass and Classic Soft filters. The Look: The look of “A Safe Distance” was heavily inspired by early New Hollywood genre cinema, and in particular the work of Vilmos Zsigmond. To that end, we employed an amusingly convoluted series of formulas involving an ever-changing mix of lens diffusion filters and film emulation looks. The goal was to progressively dirty up the image as the lead character leaves civilization for a world of wilderness and crime: the film starts in the realm of Kodak Vision3 500T and lightly applied Classic Soft filtration before gradually transitioning into a bleached-out mix of various expired Kodak and Fujifilm stocks and heavy Glimmerglass filtration. We ended up with about a dozen various permutations of filter strengths (we often stacked both types) that we chose on a scene-by-scene basis, and those were paired with eleven variations between the two extremes of our show look in the final colour grade. Our concerns with our camera and lens selections were largely practical: we needed a lightweight parfocal zoom lens that wouldn’t represent a burden in the forest, and so the Fujinon lens was a pragmatic choice. With it, we followed a rule on set: if the zoom lens was on the camera, I was allowed to improvise zooms on the spot at any point. This contributed to the film’s rough, fly-by-the-seat-of-one’s-pants aesthetic. The Venice was chosen as our A-camera for its low-light capabilities for shooting faux-firelight scenes, and the Blackmagic Pocket 6K and A7IV were on hand as lightweight B and crash cameras that matched easily to our A-cam. Digital tools, in particular, are central to my workflow: Being able to take the raw camera files home at night, bash them around in DaVinci Resolve, send out tests to the director and department heads, and create increasingly nuanced show LUTs as the shoot progresses has become a gigantic boon to our ability to experiment with the look of any given film in ever more radical ways. Dir: Lauren Noll, DP: Nathaniel Krause Category: Spotlight Format: 5K REDCODE RAW Camera: Two RED Gemini S35’s Lenses: Sigma Cine Primes and Sigma Cine Zooms The Look: For “Same Same But Different,” the camera package really grew out of the personality of the film itself. It’s a romantic comedy about growth, acceptance, and cultural collision — sometimes tender and introspective, sometimes completely unhinged. The tools had to feel as fluid as the characters and flexible enough to support the film’s wide emotional range. One of the first conversations Lauren and I had centered on whether we could realistically shoot with two cameras within our limited budget. Knowing she wanted the cast to have space to explore without sacrificing coverage, it made sense to build the production around my own gear: a RED Gemini paired with Sigma Cine lenses, with a duplicate package rented. Beyond logistics, however, the Gemini also felt right for the tonal scope of the story. Its dual native ISO gave us confidence moving from bright, sun-soaked beach sequences to intimate, low-light conversations without losing texture or subtlety. There’s a softness in the shadows that supports the vulnerability at the core of the film. The Sigma lenses complemented that tonal range beautifully — clean but not sterile, expressive without drawing attention to themselves. They are fairly fast, sharp, and neutral without being clinical, which gave us additional flexibility in post without committing to an overly baked-in look. Ultimately, the package aligned with the heart of the film: a story about identity caught between cultures, love entangled with circumstance, and friendship as the grounding force when everything else is in motion. The camera needed to hold both the absurdity of a green card wedding weekend and the intimacy of three women coming into their own — finding emotional truth in the middle of chaos. Dir: Kevin Hamedani, DP: Jon Keng Category: Spotlight Format: Sony 8.6K X-OCN Camera: Sony Venice 2 Lenses: Cineovision Anamorphics, Technovision Anamorphic Zooms, Atlas Orion Anamorphics The Look: Kevin and I were inspired by his favorite horror movies, in particular, John Carpenter’s “Halloween.” We wanted our cinematography to be bold and a modern tribute to Dean Cundey’s work. Much like “Halloween,” most of our film takes place in one house, and the lenses were crucial for creating visually arresting shots in otherwise ordinary locations. I chose the Cineovision/Technovision family specifically because of the zooms, which were important as voyeurism was one of the themes of the film. Old Fast Glass was able to hook us up with a stellar pair of Technovision zooms, which became the centerpiece of our storytelling. Dir: Aisha Evelyna, DP: Ian Carleton Category: Competition Format: Open Gate matted to 1.66:1 Camera: ALEXA Mini Lenses: Zeiss Super Speed MK III The Look: At this point in my indie film journey, everything is about getting the team more takes. The more time we can spend exploring together, the more in tune we can get with the director’s vision. On “Seahorse,” I chose to pull focus myself while operating, using an old-school manual follow focus and vintage glass. It’s an approach I’ve incorporated in my practice for years, both on narrative projects and in my daily Camera Roll photography zine, so it’s become second nature. Practically speaking, this means the resources of a focus puller can be shared with the team. An extra person on the art team or setting up lights can be the difference between two takes or three. But keeping this film sharp was unique. Aisha and I both knew the story required the core Toronto experience of biking through the hot sweaty city. Again, indie film, so no trailers or stabilized arms, but beyond that, we had an intentional visual language, part of it being handheld and intimate. A week before production, I made an all-or-nothing decision. Our PM lent me her roller skating pads and sourced a OneWheel (!) on a favour. On Day 1 of production, everyone nervously watching, I clipped into the EasyRig, took a wobbly first step onto the Onewheel, and Aisha and I rode off from the crew. As mentioned, you only get a handful of takes with indie films, so those first few riding shots are actually in the film, manual focus and all! This amalgamation of tools wonderfully captured the narrative. Biking through the city is a class story in itself, and I love how our working-class approach, which caused me at times to frantically weave with the main character, gifts the audience an authentic experience — process of creation driving art. While editing, Aisha casually noted there wasn’t a single take we had to scrap because of focus. That meant a lot to me. Dir: Victoria Strouse, DP: Tim Suhrstedt Category: Spotlight Format: 4.6K Super 35, 2.39:1 Aspect Ratio Camera: Sony Venice 2 Lenses: Angenieux Optimo Primes & Zooms The Look: As DP for Victoria Strouse, a writer and first-time director who wrote one of the best-structured, intelligent, and funny screenplays for an adult comedy that I’ve ever read, I wanted the film to feel naturalistic, fluid, and rich-looking. Our shooting schedule was only 20 days, with many locations, and driving shots (both practical and on an LED stage). For digital cinematography and for a theatrical feature, I try to make the images filmic. The Angenieux Optimo Primes are sharp but not harsh, have a low contrast, and are perfectly matched to the Optimo Zooms. We shot two cameras at all times, and, with the exception of night exteriors, we could have a zoom on one of the two cameras, which added some efficiency in terms of quick focal length changes. Victoria and I agreed that the film should be shot in the 2.39:1 aspect ratio. The story is about three adult siblings and another character on a road trip, and knowing that we would want shots with several characters in the shot, some of which are in a minivan, I knew that the wide-screen frame could accomplish this. Comedy plays very well in wide and medium shots, but the director and editor need coverage and choices in the editing room to fine-tune the rhythm of the film. You cannot be certain on the set which scenes play well in wide or medium shots, and which scenes need coverage to shape in the editing. A big part of a cinematographer’s job is to give the director the best selection of shots to work with. Dir: Russell Goldman, DP: Gemma Doll-Grossman Category: Competition Format: X-OCN-ST 4K Camera: 2x Sony Venice 1 (Rialto mode), GoPro Hero 12 Black Lenses: Standard Baltars, Laowa 12mm, Angenieux 18.5mm, Laowa periscope probe The Look: Cardboard boxes dictated the cinematography of “Sender.” Our protagonist Julia (Britt Lower) is three weeks sober. She’s doing lots of online shopping, but starts to receive unwanted items that soon trigger painful memories and a growing sense that she is being watched. With each new package, Julia’s paranoia spikes. Cardboard has never been so fraught. Director Russell Goldman and I sought to craft a visual approach that feels like the perspective of a hidden (but imaginary) camera. The high-angle/low-angle, omniscient perspectives are symptomatic of Julia’s paranoiac worldview. The camera remains stubbornly static (No pans! No tilts!), unless it’s rigged to an everyday object — a tube of lipstick, a pill bottle, a cardboard box — which offers the impression of surveillance. Shout-out to Danny, Tim, Max, Brandon, Sophie, and Bianca at Becine and the art crew for troubleshooting all of these rigs. The collaboration was truly interdepartmental. Naturally, we opted for Sony Venice cameras in Rialto mode, because the camera is both excellent quality and compact. Our lenses are similarly compact (Standard Baltars) and, when wide open, get funky and hazy, just like Julia’s psyche. Most of the film is captured with 12mm and 18.5mm lenses, which emulated a security camera’s perspective and gave our protagonist a wide canvas to play. And, undoubtedly, our aspect ratio is boxy, 3:2. Dirs: Daisy Rosato, Nora Kaye, DP: Daisy Rosato Category: Visions Format: 4K ProRes Camera: iPhone 15 Pro Lenses: Moment 19mm lens for principle photography; native iPhone lens for underwater photography The Look: Shooting an improvised feature in six days requires delusion and a swiftness in all aspects, and the camera was no different. Deciding that the iPhone would be our camera was one of the first decisions Nora and I made. It’s an homage to the lo-fi cameras of the Dogme 95 and mumblecore/gore movements, but, like those, it was also a financial decision. Not only is the iPhone’s price point lower than traditional cinema cameras, but its smaller footprint lends itself to experimentation. We were able to dive into the ocean, mount to a bike, shoot guerrilla scenes in NYC without being spotted, put the camera in a box, the freezer, the shower… You name it, we probably put the camera in it. “Sinner Supper Club” is a film about the disintegration of a friend group, about the things and people that haunt us, and the messiness of community. Shooting this way also put me directly into the fray of our characters’ psychology; I was able to move quickly, keeping up as our actors made discoveries through improvisation. The result is a visceral and immersive experience, unafraid of the iPhone’s rougher edges and our own. People loooove to say, “You have an iPhone, just shoot your movie!” so we did. Dir: Jenna MacMillan, DP: Kevin A Fraser Category: Competition Format: 4.6K 3:2 Open Gate Prores 4444 Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: DZO Pavo 2X Anamorphic, Hawk modified Angenieux 48-580mm The Look: From the images Jenna responded to in our earliest meetings, it was apparent that anamorphic glass would provide the right character for “The Snake.” We didn’t want the lenses to be optically perfect; they needed to have flaws and imperfections like our characters in the film. Many of the character anamorphics I’ve used, like the Lomos & Hawks, have a somewhat far minimum focus distance. We wanted to do long, dynamic takes where the camera is dancing, with the performers at times moving very close to them, so having to cut to put in a diopter was not going to fly. Searching for the right lenses, I found the DZO Pavos, which I had not previously known. They were fairly new at the time, and it was hard to get our hands on a set. Through creative means, we ended up with a mixed set of lenses coming from several vendors. Some were the blue flare versions, others the neutral flare. This was a happy accident for me; the mixed set of relatively cheap, lightweight lenses lent to the scrappy punk-rock aesthetic of the film and our approach to shooting it. We also had a Hawk modified 48-580mm Angenieux zoom because we wanted to shoot select scenes from a far distance with a huge amount of lens compression. I felt this made it feel like Jamie was both being observed/judged by outsiders and that she was somewhat trapped in her situation at those times in the film. The ALEXA 35 was an easy choice, and we didn’t seriously consider any other body. I’d shot several movies with it previously and knew it could handle any dynamic range, and the ISO is flexible without much image sacrifice. It’s important to me to not have to think too much about the camera during production, it frees me up to be more present with the director & cast so I can focus on the heart of each scene. Dir: Fergus Campbell, DP: Keldon Duane-McGlashan Category: Spotlight Format: ARRIRAW 4K 16:9 Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Bausch & Lomb Super Baltars, Tokina Cinema 11-20mm T2.9 The Look: Director Fergus Campbell and I wanted to create a visual language that captured the stillness of a teenage summer in Reno while hinting at the film’s magical realism. The Super Baltars provided a ton of character — so much so that shooting wide open was reserved for a select few scenes where soft, hazy edges complemented the story. We established early in prep that we would avoid excessively shallow depth of field, quick cuts, and instead shoot long, wide takes at a higher f-stop to capture the ensemble cast in reaction to one another. The Baltars maintained their character even as we stopped up, perfect for daytime desert scenes where we desired clarity in the rich backgrounds. We shot on my ALEXA 35, utilizing the soft nostalgic grain setting and, of course, relying on its dynamic range for bright exteriors. Gaffer Nica Fazio and Grip Ryan Moore were beyond incredible, testing their expertise against sand, hail, rain, and mud. Every person on this “Sparks” crew worked so hard to bring the movie to life, and for that I am forever grateful! Dir: Joe Swanberg, DP: Eon Mora Category: Spotlight Format: 35mm Camera: Panavision Millenium XL2, DJI Inspire 3 Lenses: Panavision Primo Classic Series Primes The Look: I have always been fond of these lenses as workhorses for the 35mm format. I feel like the represent as the high-water mark for Panavision’s engineering, as well as retaining a ton of character without loss of sharpness. Bringing the Alaskan landscape into the image was a major priority for this project, so choosing a lens that could reproduce the naturalism we were going for, without editorializing too much, was a goal. I love the way that they flair, something that plays a major role in the imagery of this film, with the perpetual sunlight throughout. They offer a simple but profoundly organic look that I really appreciate. Dir: Stella Marie Markert, DPs: Edgar Fischnaller and Jonas Kolahdoozan Category: Global Format: 4.8k Open Gate ProRes4444 Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Zeiss Standard Speeds, Canon 16mm Zoom Lens The Look: Authenticity guided every decision in our cinematography. The film follows four girls sharing a flat in Berlin’s Prenzlauer Berg, each with a vibrant and distinct personality. From the beginning, it was important that the visual language would never overshadow these characters. Our goal was to create a camera style that feels present and alive while allowing the performances and relationships to remain at the center of the film. We shot on Zeiss Standard Speed lenses and relied largely on natural lighting to achieve an image that feels organic and unpolished. The character of the lenses complemented the emotional honesty of the story and supported the sometimes chaotic energy of the four protagonists without imposing a heavy visual style. The camera is mostly handheld and constantly moving, subtly breathing with the characters and the rhythm of their interactions. Rather than observing from a distance, the camera exists within their shared space. As the story progresses and the bond between the girls deepens, the camera gradually moves closer. Early in the film, our close-ups remain slightly wider, maintaining a sense of distance. As trust grows between the characters, the camera becomes more intimate, reflecting their emotional closeness. For the sequences exploring the characters’ childhoods, we wanted a visual distinction without changing the aspect ratio. We imagined these moments as point-and-shoot photographs from a summer vacation. To create a flatter and more two-dimensional quality, we used the ALEXA 35 in its 16mm mode paired with a 16mm zoom lens. Dir: Katie Aselton, DP: Sarah Whelden Category: Spotlight Format: 6K RAW Lite Camera: Canon C400 & C80 Lenses: Canon CN-E The Look: My first conversation with director Katie Aselton centered around building an intimate set with an extremely minimal footprint, creating a space as raw and vulnerable as the story we were telling, and allowing us to pivot quickly if inspiration struck. Practically speaking, I needed a camera package that was flexible enough to handle the plethora of locations, including all of the expansive night exteriors, but that was small and nimble enough to not slow down our extremely small team. We landed on two Canon C400s for our handheld and tripod rigs, with a C80 setup full-time on a small gimbal. I already loved Canon’s amazing color science and knew they would be a great fit ergonomically for the films, but the triple base ISO proved to be a game-changer for me. I loved how quickly I could shift my dynamic range for a consistent look as we moved around the streets of Bangor, Maine, with very little lighting equipment. The CN-Es gave us a very modern look with beautiful flares and just enough character when opened up. Their wide aperture allowed us to really isolate our actors, letting the world fade away as their relationship with each other blossoms throughout the film. Dir: Emily Robinson, DP: Brody S. Anderson Category: Spotlight Format: 4.6K ProRes Camera: ALEXA 35 Lenses: Zeiss Super Speeds, Angenieux 35-140mm The Look: Emily — as the writer, director, and lead — had a very clear sense of the story she wanted to share, so I felt my function was to just help her tell it as efficiently as possible. Knowing we were going to be tight on time, we devised a simple shooting strategy and assembled a camera package that let us zip through our days. We landed on the ALEXA 35 combined with Zeiss Super Speeds, which allowed our gaffer, Paul Kim, and me to use a mix of natural light and smaller sources without compromising the exposure we wanted. Emily and I believed a few key scenes needed to be long, uninterrupted zooms, but I didn’t want the burden of a heavy lens, so Dan Thomas and Kevin Kenney at Eastside Camera pointed us to an old Angeniuex zoom they call Cricket. It weighs about 2lbs and has a great range. It paired nicely with the primes, and its tiny size allowed our first and only AC, Riona Oshima-Ryan (a godsend), to swap back and forth quickly. Honestly, I’m not so sure the gear had a huge impact on the film. It all helped, of course, but I think equipment gets a little overemphasized, and I’d love to shift attention back to Emily, the cast, and the crew. People make movies. Thanks for spending a sweaty spring in Toluca Lake with me! Dir: Graham Parkes, DP: Christopher Ripley Category: Competition Format: Sony X-OCN Camera: Sony Venice 2 Lenses: Zeiss Super Speeds, Compact Angenieux Optimo Zooms The Look: It was really important for our world to feel alive and messy. Although the film plays with fantastical ideas, the emotional throughline is very human, so we wanted our visual language to be grounded. Zeiss Super Speeds and compact Angenieux Optimos were perfect lenses for that. They’re lightweight, which was great for our handheld shooting style, and they tend to render images with a straightforward functionality that feels lived-in instead of commercially clean or overly vintage and affected. My crew lit close to 360-degree sets, mainly using practicals and a few small hidden units. We wanted the actors to have the freedom to move around and inhabit the space fully, and for our camera to be able to explore new angles and lively moments with every take — all while maintaining our shooting pace and avoiding relighting. The director Graham and I shamelessly called it “Supermarket Sweep:” A hungry, chaotic, playful camera that strove to capture lightning in a bottle, even with our fast 20-day shooting schedule. We shot Sony Venice 2, which I think has a lovely color science and rendering of detail. With the right film emulation and color pipeline, the Venice 2 has a transparent quality to it that feels more like a photochemical print from the late ‘90s or early 2000s. That “ungraded 35mm” image felt well-suited to our grounded style of storytelling. By providing your information, you agree to our Terms of Use and our Privacy Policy. Title: Thanks To The War In Iran, A Moment Of Reckoning Has Arrived For The Entire Global Economy
URL: https://www.shtfplan.com/economics/thanks-to-the-war-in-iran-a-moment-of-reckoning-has-arrived-for-the-entire-global-economy
Time Published: 2026-03-14T17:00:40Z
Full Content:
by Michael Snyder | Mar 14, 2026 | Economics | 0 comments This article was originally published by Michael Snyder at The Economic Collapse Blog. Global financial markets are starting to shake, and it is because of what is happening in the Middle East. At this stage, every day is a rollercoaster ride for investors, and that is not likely to change any time soon. The Iranians have paralyzed traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, and trying to reopen it by force is going to be exceptionally difficult. As you will see below, this is going to have enormous implications for every nation on the entire planet. We are in the early chapters of a major global crisis, and a tremendous amount of pain is ahead. On Thursday, Mojtaba Khamenei issued a statement in which he boldly declared that the Strait of Hormuz must remain closed in order to “pressure the enemy”… Iran’s new supreme leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, said Thursday that the closure of the Strait of Hormuz maritime passage should be continued as a “tool to pressure the enemy,” in his first public statement since being appointed. Khamenei also said all U.S. military bases in the Middle East should close immediately and “those bases will be attacked,” in televised comments translated by Reuters. Oil prices extended gains following the statement, read out by a state TV broadcaster. The shipping of oil through the Strait of Hormuz has effectively stopped since the war began, causing global oil prices to soar. Iran warned on Wednesday that the price per barrel could climb to $200. Needless to say, the Iranians are fully capable of carrying out Khamenei’s threats. Several more vessels have been attacked within the past 48 hours, and that includes a U.S.-owned oil tanker that was completely destroyed… Iran’s semi-official Tasnim news agency also released a dramatic video apparently showing a missile strike on a U.S.-owned tanker in the Persian Gulf. The video shows a massive explosion on a vessel that is then engulfed in flames as people aboard what appears to be an IRGC attack boat celebrate. CBS News Confirmed identified the vessel as the Safesea Vishnu, a crude oil tanker sailing under a Marshall Islands flag, but owned by the New Jersey-based company Safesea Group LLC. Now that Iran has officially closed the Strait of Hormuz, it will be up to the U.S. military to try to reopen it. This will not be an easy task. In fact, some military experts are warning that it could take months to accomplish that goal… Unblocking the Strait of Hormuz is shaping up as a remarkably complex and time-consuming task. The fate of the global oil markets—and the global economy—rests on whether ships can traverse the 22-mile-wide waterway from the Persian Gulf to the Gulf of Oman. More than 10 days after the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran, there is no clear plan for reopening it. Military experts say the effort is so daunting it could take months to achieve, absent a fast and full cease-fire. So exactly why will it be so difficult to reopen the Strait of Hormuz? As one expert has aptly noted, the Iranians are able to block traffic through the Strait in a multitude of different ways… The problem is that the strait can’t be cleared solely through a bombing campaign, or an attack by destroyers. Iran’s navy has been severely depleted by the U.S., but the country doesn’t need large attack ships to scare commercial vessels away. Its weapons include mines, fast attack boats, missiles and drones. Layered on top of each other, they become exceptionally difficult to remove. “They’ve created an integrated, vertical stack of threats that can cover the strait from undersea all the way to above the surface,” said Jonathan Schroden, chief research officer at the Center for Naval Analyses, a federally funded nonprofit that does research for the Navy. If you take out all of their missiles, they would still have thousands of drones. If you could somehow locate and eliminate all of the drones, they would still have their fast attack boats. And even if you could eliminate all of their fast attack boats, they would still have thousands of naval mines. My point is that this crisis is not going to be resolved easily. And that is really, really bad news for the global economy. Right now, approximately half of the available global fleet of liquified natural gas carriers is trapped in the Persian Gulf… According to the WSJ, at least 20 LNG carriers about half the available global fleet – are trapped in the Persian Gulf, with daily freight costs soaring as demand from Asia surges, according to ship brokers. This is a nightmare. Liquified natural gas prices have already soared in Europe and in Asia, and this is probably only just the beginning… Ship brokers said the 20 ships trapped in the Persian Gulf make up nearly half of all LNG ships currently available for charter, with daily rates rising to more than $200,000 from less than $98,000 before the start of the Iran hostilities. Energy traders expect LNG prices to rise by early next week, adding to this week’s 40% rise in Asia and Europe. “The effect on LNG shipping will outlast the conflict for a few months,” Karathanos said. Amid the scramble to procure LNG, more shipments bound for Europe are diverting to Asia. At least nine cargoes initially headed to Europe have changed course to Asia since the start of the fighting, according to ship-tracking data compiled by Bloomberg, with the trend accelerating in recent days. A buffer of spare supply is quickly drying up, threatening more competition and higher prices for both regions. Meanwhile, a very large percentage of the nitrogen fertilizer that famers in the northern hemisphere are counting on is also stuck in the Persian Gulf region… Nearly half the world’s traded urea — the most widely used nitrogen fertilizer — comes from the Gulf region, with Qatar accounting for one-tenth of the global supply, according to Bloomberg Intelligence. When QatarEnergy last week halted production after Iranian strikes on Ras Laffan, the world’s biggest LNG and fertilizer hub, hundreds of thousands of tons of key fertilizer nutrients and precursors were sidelined. The compounding effects of the Iran war threaten the third major risk to global food security in six years, after the COVID-19 pandemic and Moscow’s seizure of farmland and ports used to export Ukrainian grain at the start of Russia’s war in Ukraine in 2022. If we can’t get that fertilizer into the hands of the farmers that need it, we are going to have a massive problem on our hands. Approximately half of the entire population of the world eats food that is grown using nitrogen fertilizer… About 4 billion people on the planet eat food grown with synthetic nitrogen fertilizers. Roughly half of the global population, in other words, is alive because of these chemicals converted into nutrients for plants, said Lorenzo Rosa, who researches sustainable energy, water, and food systems at the Carnegie Institution for Science at Stanford University. If this war persists for an extended period of time, famine will become a major global theme. Of course the war with Iran has also caused the greatest oil supply disruption in human history… The war in the Middle East is creating the largest supply disruption in the history of the global oil market, the International Energy Agency said in a report on Thursday slashing its annual supply outlook, shortly after the group announced its biggest-ever oil stock release. It is now being projected that the price of oil could soon hit $150 a barrel, and that would absolutely crush the global economy… If it remains fully closed for weeks, analysts see oil prices climbing to $150—a level that could send the U.S. and other economies into recession. Countries are going to great lengths—from emptying their strategic oil stockpiles to rerouting supplies through underutilized pipelines—to find other ways to replace the shortage. None of those ideas will come close to replacing all the lost supplies. Meanwhile, trouble is brewing in the financial markets. In fact, George Noble is warning that a major crisis is emerging in the private credit market… George Noble, a longtime Fidelity fund manager and Wall Street veteran, sees big problems brewing in the booming private credit market. The former director of Fidelity Overseas Fund recently wrote that he sees the makings of a financial crisis in the sector, echoing others who have worried about a spate of negative headlines in recent months. So what is it specifically that has him so spooked? Well, it is the fact that so many big players are starting to block investors from withdrawing their money… “We’re watching a financial crisis unfold in real time,” he said in a post on X. “The last time funds started blocking investors from getting their money back, Bear Stearns collapsed six months later.” The collapse of the investment bank is often seen as one of the first dominoes of the 2008 financial crisis. Noble pointed to recent news of redemptions at major firms, including BlackRock, Blackstone, and Blue Owl. Noble is right. We haven’t seen anything like this since 2008. And now we can add Morgan Stanley to the list… Morgan Stanley’s investment management arm became the latest asset manager to cap withdrawals from a private credit fund, telling investors in a letter Wednesday evening that it saw a wave of redemption requests this quarter. The company’s North Haven Private Income Fund, which has $7.6 billion in total investments, received investor requests to buy back around 10.9% of outstanding shares in its quarterly tender offer. The fund capped repurchases at 5%, its previously disclosed threshold. “By maintaining appropriate limits on the quarterly repurchase offer, the [fund] seeks to avoid asset sales during periods of market dislocation and provide for conservative capital structure management through evolving market conditions,” the letter said. This is it guys. What we have been waiting for has officially begun. I hope that you are ready for what is coming next, because the months ahead are going to be absolutely filled with chaos. Michael’s new book entitled “10 Prophetic Events That Are Coming Next” is available in paperback and for the Kindle on Amazon.com, and you can subscribe to his Substack newsletter at michaeltsnyder.substack.com. When you purchase any of Michael’s books, you help to support the work that he is doing. You can also get his articles by email as soon as he publishes them by subscribing to his Substack newsletter. Michael has published thousands of articles on The Economic Collapse Blog, End Of The American Dream, and The Most Important News, and he always freely and happily allows others to republish those articles on their own websites. These are such troubled times, and people need hope. John 3:16 tells us about the hope that God has given us through Jesus Christ: “For God so loved the world, that he gave his only begotten Son, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life.” If you have not already done so, we strongly urge you to invite Jesus Christ to be your Lord and Savior today. Title: Digital dollar power balance cracks as Circle’s growth spurt closes in on Tether’s dominance
URL: https://cryptoslate.com/the-digital-dollar-power-balance-cracks-as-circles-growth-spurt-closes-in-on-tethers-dominance/
Time Published: 2026-03-14T16:10:13Z
Full Content:
USDC supply surged 8% in a month, tightening Circle’s challenge to Tether’s long-held stablecoin dominance. Also known as "Akiba," Liam Wright is a reporter, podcast producer, and Editor-in-Chief at CryptoSlate. He believes that decentralized technology has the potential to make widespread positive change. A quiet shift is underway in the stablecoin hierarchy. While Tether’s USDT still dominates the digital dollar market, the gap between the two largest issuers is narrowing as USDC steadily expands its footprint and Tether’s growth shows signs of softening. Additionally, USDC is gaining ground in the places where the next wave of crypto money is likely to show up most clearly: regulated payments, institutional settlement, and high-velocity on-chain transfers. Tether’s USDT still holds the largest stock of digital dollars in circulation, but the contest is shifting from a simple market-cap race to a fight over which issuer controls the rails that move new capital through crypto. That split is now visible in both the long-term structure and the last month of market-cap movement. The stablecoin market stands at about $315 billion, giving the sector a much larger base than earlier in the cycle. Within that pool, USDT still leads with 58% market share by supply, keeping Tether firmly in command of the largest crypto cash reserve. Cross-border flows have finally overtaken Ethereum, proving these tokens are no longer just for crypto gambling. Supply, however, is only one part of the picture. The more revealing question is where fresh dollars are going, which token they move through, and which issuer is building infrastructure institutions can use at scale. That is where Circle has started to build a stronger case. Circle's financial statements confirm USDC circulation reached $75 billion at the end of 2025, up 72% year over year, while Q4 on-chain transaction volume climbed to $12 trillion, up 247% from a year earlier. Those figures indicate a stablecoin moving through wallets, venues, and payment flows more quickly. Tether, for its part, remains too large to dismiss. In its latest quarterly disclosure, Tether stated USDT circulation topped $186 billion, reserve assets approached $193 billion, and its total US Treasury exposure reached $141 billion. It also said it issued nearly $50 billion in new USDT during 2025. Those figures show a business that still dominates the inventory side of crypto dollars, especially across exchanges, offshore trading venues, and markets where users want a dollar-linked asset without relying on local banking systems. Over the past month, USDC’s market cap has risen around 8%, pushing it to roughly $79 billion and a fresh all-time high. Tether has remained far larger, but USDT is still sitting about $3 billion below the roughly $187 billion peak it reached in December 2025, a gap that gives Circle a clearer opening to chip away at Tether’s lead than the headline supply table alone suggests. So the tension is real. Tether still controls the biggest pile of crypto cash. Circle is building faster in the parts of the market most closely aligned with the next phase of regulation and institutional adoption. Despite a downgrade from S&P, Tether's gold and Bitcoin stockpile is seen as strength by crypto markets. For traders and Bitcoin investors, stablecoins remain the main form of dollar liquidity inside crypto. Whoever captures more of the next inflow can shape where liquidity thickens, how collateral is posted, and which rails become the default path for new capital entering the market. The cleanest way to understand the shift is to separate supply from velocity. USDT still leads in outstanding supply, meaning more dollars are parked in Tether than in any rival stablecoin. But transaction data suggests USDC is gaining influence over how money moves. Bloomberg, citing Artemis Analytics, reported that stablecoin transaction volume rose 72% to $33 trillion in 2025, with USDC accounting for $18.3 trillion and USDT for $13.3 trillion. That divergence carries more weight than a simple supply table. A stablecoin that wins more transaction flow can become the preferred medium for settlement, treasury movement, and short-duration capital rotation, even while another token still holds a larger long-term balance. Put differently, Tether still looks stronger as stored crypto cash, while Circle is making a case to become the preferred token for moving crypto cash. The market is also assigning the two issuers different jobs. Tether’s edge remains distribution. It has the deepest footprint across global exchanges and a large user base in emerging markets, where demand for dollar-linked assets often reflects local currency weakness, capital controls, or banking friction. Circle’s edge is legibility. It has built a reserve model and disclosure framework that fit more naturally with banks, regulated payment firms, and institutions that need cleaner lines around custody, compliance, and audits. Circle’s own transparency page makes that pitch directly. The company says the bulk of USDC reserves sit in the BlackRock-managed Circle Reserve Fund, with the rest primarily in cash at regulated financial institutions, and notes that its financial statements are audited by Deloitte. That does not erase market competition, and it does not guarantee that USDC will overtake USDT by supply. It does give Circle a stronger position in the regulated lane of the market at a moment when regulation is beginning to sort winners by use case. The law is signed, but the real fight starts in rulemaking and phased rollouts through 2026–27. Here’s the timeline, the winners, and how flows could reroute crypto rails. The policy backdrop is moving in that direction. A Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis review of the GENIUS Act framework says payment stablecoin issuers face tight reserve rules, monthly disclosures, and annual audited financial statements once issuance passes $50 billion. State-qualified issuers above $10 billion would also need to move toward federal oversight within a year. Those thresholds do not decide the market on their own, but they make compliance architecture more important than it was during the earlier, more crypto-native phase of stablecoin growth. That split is already visible in payments. Visa launched USDC settlement in the United States with Cross River Bank and Lead Bank and plans broader U.S. expansion through 2026. It also said its monthly stablecoin settlement volume had reached a $3.5 billion annualized run rate as of November 30. Market-moving headlines and context delivered every morning in one tight read. Free. No spam. Unsubscribe any time. That is not the same as saying USDC will dominate all crypto activity. Circle, however, is gaining share in one of the most important growth lanes outside exchange trading. For Bitcoin, the stablecoin contest is not a side issue. Stablecoins fund exchange balances, back collateral positions, and give traders a dollar-linked unit that can move around the clock without leaving the crypto system. When stablecoin supply grows, the market’s pool of deployable dollar liquidity tends to deepen. When one stablecoin gains more of that growth, the question becomes which venues and user groups will control the new liquidity. Glassnode has described the Stablecoin Supply Ratio as a gauge of stablecoin-denominated buying power relative to Bitcoin supply, with lower readings implying greater potential purchasing power. That supports a practical point: stablecoins are one of the clearest ways to measure how much dollar liquidity is sitting inside crypto and how ready that liquidity may be to rotate into BTC. If USDT remains the main store of offshore trading cash while USDC gains ground in regulated settlement and enterprise finance, Bitcoin liquidity could become more segmented over the next year. Offshore spot and derivatives venues may remain heavily USDT-centric. Meanwhile, institutionally mediated Bitcoin activity could lean more toward USDC as banks, payment firms, and treasury desks choose the stablecoin that best fits compliance, reserve transparency, and settlement requirements. That would not weaken Bitcoin. Tether would still matter most for the largest reservoir of crypto-native trading capital, and it could broaden the set of rails that feed Bitcoin demand. Circle would matter more for the next tranche of regulated capital seeking a stablecoin bridge to digital assets without stepping outside traditional financial guardrails. Standard Chartered has projected that the stablecoin market could reach $2 trillion by the end of 2028. From a base of roughly $315 billion today, that implies about $1.7 trillion of additional room for growth. The key question is which issuer, reserve model, and regulatory framework will capture the next $1.7 trillion. There are several plausible paths from here. The evidence today supports the first path more than the others. Tether is still too large, too embedded, and too useful across crypto’s global trading stack to call this an imminent overthrow. USYC hit $1.69B AUM on Jan. 22, narrowly topping BUIDL’s $1.684B. The real advantage is buried in collateral rails. Circle, though, has enough momentum in transactions, reserve design, and institutional integrations to argue that the next phase of stablecoin growth may not belong to the same issuer that dominated the last one. Circle’s case also rests on recency, not just structure. USDC has hit a new market-cap high near $79 billion after roughly 8% monthly growth, while USDT has yet to reclaim the peak it reached in December 2025. The broader takeaway for Bitcoin and the wider market is straightforward. USDT still owns the largest share of crypto’s cash inventory. USDC is making a stronger claim on crypto’s future cash plumbing. If stablecoins are heading toward a multi-trillion-dollar market, the fight is no longer just about who is biggest now. It is about who captures the next wave of money, and which version of the dollar becomes the preferred bridge into Bitcoin, exchanges, payments, and on-chain finance. Switch categories to dive deeper or gain broader context. With Deloitte's name attached to USAT, Tether gains a US-regulated spotlight without resolving USDT audit demands. Tether is betting that the next risk-off rush will run through USDT, XAU₮, and a familiar retail gold checkout. Citi still sees upside for Bitcoin and Ethereum, but slower US policy is shrinking how far prices can run this year. Bitcoin will finally face the pain Satoshi wrote about in the first block transaction before the next halving. Trady's zero-friction architecture enables seamless cross-chain trading with up to 1% rebates. Aster is broadening its WLFI partnership to include perpetual markets settled in USD1, enhancing liquidity and trading options for users. The cuts come as the technology company aims to offset the cost of artificial intelligence infrastructure and prepare for greater efficiency brought about by AI-assisted workers, three sources familiar with the matter told Reuters. The outlet added that the timing and size of the potential layoffs have not been finalized. When reached for comment, a Meta spokesperson told FOX Business, "This is a speculative report about theoretical approaches." $0.00212938 $0.00563515 $22.99 $0.0254296 $1.25 $0.206253 $1.001 $0.209307 $1.19 $0.00203326 $0.054893 $1.077 $2.34 $2.69 $1.00 $1.067 $0.059882 $0.102593 $0.080581 $18.65 $0.01932875 $1.00 $0.17668 $0.091273 $2.25 $1.001 $1.91 $0.174763 $0.04490143 $0.071548 $1.79 $0.098417 $0.607533 $48.00 $0.00337078 $0.99513 $4,132.28 $0.155661 $1.23 $1.002 $0.099706 $0.423541 $0.511135 $0.326941 $0.169621 $0.0199746 $0.28741 $0.00130203 $0.00000693 $1.085 $0.097406 $0.589224 $0.00000067 $0.133508 $0.0222722 $1.02 $0.233662 $1,091.33 $0.282611 $0.079167 $0.412054 $0.199483 $0.286539 $0.998657 $0.999239 $0.999883 $0.312929 $0.999402 $0.556492 $0.129171 $0.0000009 $4.11 $1.004 $0.548594 $1.50 $0.547434 $0.00628951 $1.00 $1.74 $1.41 $2.26 $0.00133174 The Bitcoin network saw its 20 millionth BTC mined this week, leaving just 1 million coins left to be paid as rewards to miners. The milestone has crypto industry observers taking stock of the rapidly changing Bitcoin mining industry, and weighing the economics of a shifting landscape against expectations of Bitcoin’s performance as an investment. Mining companies help secure the Bitcoin network and verify transactions, expending large amounts of energy in a race to solve cryptographic puzzles in exchange for transaction fees and newly created Bitcoin as rewards. It’s taken miners 16 years to mine the 20 millionth coin from Bitcoin’s inception, but it could take roughly 115 years to unlock the remaining supply, according to Wolfie Zhao, the head of research at TheEnergyMag. That doesn’t necessarily mean the Bitcoin mining industry will look the way it does for the next century. John Todaro, a managing director and senior research analyst at Needham & Company, expects many publicly traded miners to exit Bitcoin mining in 2027 and 2028. “​​We believe a large portion of the public Bitcoin miners will sell down nearly all of their Bitcoin holdings before year-end 2026 as they embark on [capital expenditure] spend related to AI workloads,” he wrote in a recent note shared with Decrypt. In other words, Bitcoin mining companies are pivoting to AI. All the publicly traded Bitcoin miners the firm covers have allocated a portion of their compute power to high-performance computing, or HPC, and AI. It’s a shift that’s been going on for years. And it’s easy to see why, he added. “Stubbornly low hash price combined with the upcoming 2028 halving presents a concerning environment for Bitcoin mining operations,” he told Decrypt. “Many operators are at or near breakeven costs today, while NOI margins in HPC are north of 80%.” NOI refers to net operating income, which measures revenue minus operating expenses, excluding financing costs and taxes. So it stands to reason that mining firms are adjusting their revenue split to favor better margins. Ross Gan, the chief communications officer at Bitdeer, told Decrypt the firm has Bitcoin’s technological infrastructure in its DNA. Bitdeer, the Singapore-based miner led by Bitmain co-founder Jihan Wu, illustrates the fork in the road facing the industry. Wu helped industrialize Bitcoin mining in the first place—Bitmain, which he co-founded in 2013, once controlled roughly three-quarters of the global market for Bitcoin mining chips. Now Bitdeer is converting several of its facilities into AI data centers while simultaneously developing its own next-generation mining hardware. “The miners that endure will be the ones that control more of the stack themselves. We demonstrate how that matters by designing and deploying our own high-efficiency ASICs and securing long-term energy capacity worldwide,” Gan said. “Vertical integration has proven to be one of the clearest markers of long-term survivability.” He added that up until recently, Bitcoin has been treated as a key monetization engine that was complemented by AI infrastructure to keep long-term revenues stable. “That duality may no longer be a nice-to-have in the future,” Gan said. HIVE Digital Technologies, formerly HIVE Blockchain, was founded in 2017 and went public later that year on the Toronto Stock Exchange. The company began investing in high performance computing, or HPC, infrastructure much earlier than many of its competitors. So early, in fact, that it was still generating revenue from Ethereum mining when Executive Chairman Frank Holmes mentioned it on an earnings call. “The Ethereum mining margins that we experienced during the quarter enabled us to continue the upgrade of our data center assets in Sweden and Iceland and also diversify our business by starting to invest in HPC assets,” he said in November 2021. It wasn’t until a year later that Ethereum developers executed the merge, changing the network from a proof-of-work to proof-of-stake consensus mechanism and rendering Ethereum mining obsolete. The Canadian company has built its business around finding creative ways to source power from hydro-electric and otherwise stranded energy, Holmes told Decrypt. “Bitcoin miners have led the world in sourcing stranded and surplus energy and in building Tier I power infrastructure at scale,” he said. “There is enormous energy abundance in the world, especially in hydro-rich regions like South America and Canada, but the winners will be operators that can secure it at low cost, structure around it intelligently, and turn that energy into durable computing infrastructure.” Even as analysts, like Todaro, predict that some Bitcoin mining firms will begin winding down by the end of 2027, Holmes sees the squeeze ahead of the next halving event—forecast for mid-2028—as a challenge to get even more efficient. “Block rewards will decrease, but that does not mean the industry will disappear. It means the bar rises,” he added. “The miners that survive will be the ones with the best power, the best sites, and the most flexibility.” But what happens to the price of Bitcoin when block rewards get all the way to zero? Investors have known that Bitcoin has a finite supply since its inception, so theoretically it’s priced in. The most apt comparison comes from the Bitcoin whitepaper itself: “The steady addition of a [constant amount] of new coins is analogous to gold miners expending resources to add gold to circulation,” pseudonymous BTC creator Satoshi Nakamoto wrote in 2008. The comparison has been adopted widely by Bitcoin fans, including BlackRock CEO Larry Fink, Strategy founder Michael Saylor, and even Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell. The global gold supply hasn’t been exhausted yet, so investors can’t skip ahead a few chapters for a preview of what BTC might do in 115 years. But Todaro pointed out that the very gradual reduction in block rewards should dampen effects on Bitcoin’s price. He expects the majority of selling pressure to come from newly produced BTC, not long-time HODLers. And even if Bitcoin miners liquidate their holdings as they exit the business, they’re not the whales they used to be. “Bitcoin miners do not hold as much Bitcoin on their balance sheets on a relative basis as they historically have,” he said. “They hold ~0.5% of the circulating supply, while Strategy alone holds 7x more BTC than all the miners combined.” Your gateway into the world of Web3 The latest news, articles, and resources, sent to your inbox weekly. © A next-generation media company. 2026 Decrypt Media, Inc.

Full Content:

Title: Meta layoffs: Mark Zuckerberg's tech firm plans to cut nearly 16,000 jobs as AI costs mount, says report — what we know
URL: https://www.livemint.com/companies/news/meta-layoffs-mark-zuckerbergs-tech-firm-plans-to-cut-nearly-16-000-jobs-as-ai-costs-mount-says-report-what-we-know-11773451516319.html
Time Published: 2026-03-14T02:10:44Z
Full Content:
Mark Zuckerberg-led Meta is planning sweeping layoffs that could affect 20% or more of the company to offset costly artificial intelligence infrastructure bets and prepare for greater efficiency brought about by AI-assisted workers, according to a Reuters report. However, no date has been set for these cuts, and the magnitude has not been finalised, Reuters was told by sources. Top executives have recently signaled the plans to other senior leaders at Meta and told them to begin planning how to pare back, anonymous sources told Reuters. "This is speculative reporting about theoretical approaches," Meta spokesperson Andy Stone said in response to questions about the plan. Meta employed nearly 79,000 people as of December 31, according to its latest filing. If Meta settles on the 20% figure, this layoff may affect nearly 16,000 employees, making it the most significant job cut at Mark Zuckerberg's company since its restructuring in the "year of efficiency" — late 2022 and early 2023. Meta laid off 11,000 staffers in November 2022, around 13% of its workforce at the time. Around four months later, it announced it was cutting another 10,000 jobs. Over the last year, CEO Zuckerberg has been pushing Meta to compete more forcefully in generative AI. The company has offered huge pay packages, some worth hundreds of millions of dollars over four years, to court top AI researchers to a new superintelligence team. The company has said it plans to invest $600 billion to build data centres by 2028. Earlier this week, it acquired Moltbook, a social networking platform built for AI agents. Meta is also spending at least $2 billion to buy a Chinese AI startup – Manus. Zuckerberg has alluded to efficiency gains from the investments, saying in January he was starting to see "projects that used to require big teams now be accomplished by a single very talented person." Meta's planned AI investments follow a series of setbacks with its Llama 4 models last year, including criticism that it provided misleading results on the benchmarks it used for early versions. It abandoned the release of the largest version of that model, called Behemoth, which had been due out in the summer. Meta's superintelligence team has been working to reassert the company's standing this year by building a new model called Avocado, but the performance of that model has also lagged expectations. Its release has also been pushed to later. Meta's layoff plans reflect a broader pattern among major US companies, particularly in tech, this year. Executives have pointed to recent improvements in AI systems as one reason for the changes. In January, Amazon confirmed it would cut some 16,000 jobs, amounting to nearly 10% of its workforce. Last month, the fintech company Block chopped nearly half of its staff, with CEO Jack Dorsey explicitly pointing to AI tools and their growing capability to help companies do more with smaller teams. Title: Meta plans major layoffs of up to 20% amid AI investments and efficiency drive
URL: https://www.thehindubusinessline.com/info-tech/meta-plans-major-layoffs-of-up-to-20-amid-ai-investments-and-efficiency-drive/article70742164.ece
Time Published: 2026-03-14T00:36:34Z
Full Content:
-1,470.50 -488.05 + 276.00 -1,796.00 -8,665.00 -1,470.50 -488.05 -488.05 + 276.00 + 276.00 -1,796.00 CEO Mark Zuckerberg is pushing Meta to compete aggressively in generative AI, offering high-value compensation to top researchers, acquiring startups like Moltbook and Manus, and investing $600 billion in data centers by 2028 | Photo Credit: ANNEGRET HILSE Meta is planning sweeping layoffs that could affect 20% ‌or more of the company, three sources familiar with the matter told Reuters, ​as Meta seeks to offset costly artificial intelligence infrastructure bets and prepare ⁠for greater efficiency brought about by AI-assisted workers. No date has been set for the cuts and the magnitude has not been finalized, the people said. Top executives have recently signaled the plans to ‌other senior leaders at Meta and told them to begin planning how to pare back, two of the people said. The sources spoke anonymously because they ‌were not authorized to disclose the cuts. Meta did not immediately comment. If Meta ‌settles ⁠on the 20% figure, the layoffs will be the company's most significant ⁠since a restructuring in late 2022 and early 2023 that it dubbed the "year of efficiency." It employed nearly 79,000 people as of December 31, according to its latest filing. The company laid off 11,000 staffers in November ​2022, or around 13% of its workforce ‌at the time. Around four months later, it announced it was cutting another 10,000 jobs. Over the last year, CEO Mark Zuckerberg has been pushing Meta to compete more forcefully in generative AI. The company has offered huge ‌pay packages, some worth hundreds of millions of dollars over four years, to ​court top AI researchers to a new superintelligence team. The company has said it plans to invest $600 billion to build data centers by 2028. ⁠Earlier this week, it acquired Moltbook, a social networking platform built for AI agents. Meta is also spending at least $2 billion to buy Chinese AI startup Manus, Reuters previously reported. Zuckerberg has ‌alluded to efficiency gains from the investments, saying in January he was starting to see "projects that used to require big teams now be accomplished by a single very talented person." Meta's plans reflect a broader pattern among major U.S. companies, particularly in tech, this year. Executives have pointed to recent improvements in AI systems as one reason for the changes. In January, Amazon confirmed it would cut some 16,000 jobs, amounting to nearly 10% of its ‌workforce. Last month, the fintech company Block chopped nearly half of its staff, with CEO Jack Dorsey explicitly ​pointing to AI tools and their growing capability to help companies do more with smaller teams. Meta's planned AI investments follow a series of ⁠setbacks with its Llama 4 models last year, including criticism that it provided misleading results on ⁠the benchmarks it used for early versions. It abandoned the release of the largest version of that model, called Behemoth, which had been due out in ‌the summer. The superintelligence team has been working to reassert the company's standing this year by building a new model called Avocado, but the performance of that model has ​also lagged expectations.
